# Inserting data into int4range table (2026-02-18 14:37Z)

_**User**_

I created this table: create table t(r int4range);

How do I insert into it?
For a copy I can do:
COPY t FROM STDIN;
[7,10)


---

_**Agent (model default, mode Agent)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

The user is asking about how to insert into a table that has an `int4range` column. They've shown that for COPY they can use the format `[7,10)` which is the standard PostgreSQL range literal format.

Let me search for how INSERT works with range types in Materialize, and what format is expected for range literals in INSERT statements.
</details></think>

---

      "codeBlock": {

        "contents": "    }\n}\n\nimpl&lt;'a, T: FromSql&lt;'a&gt;&gt; FromSql&lt;'a&gt; for Range&lt;T&gt; {\n    fn from_sql(ty: &amp;PgType, raw: &amp;'a [u8]) -&gt; Result&lt;Range&lt;T&gt;, Box&lt;dyn Error + Sync + Send&gt;&gt; {\n        let inner_typ = match ty {\n            &amp;PgType::INT4_RANGE =&gt; PgType::INT4,\n            &amp;PgType::INT8_RANGE =&gt; PgType::INT8,\n            &amp;PgType::DATE_RANGE =&gt; PgType::DATE,\n            &amp;PgType::NUM_RANGE =&gt; PgType::NUMERIC,\n            &amp;PgType::TS_RANGE =&gt; PgType::TIMESTAMP,\n            &amp;PgType::TSTZ_RANGE =&gt; PgType::TIMESTAMPTZ,\n            _ =&gt; unreachable!(),\n        };\n\n        let inner = match types::range_from_sql(raw)? {\n            types::Range::Empty =&gt; None,\n            types::Range::Nonempty(lower, upper) =&gt; {\n                let mut bounds = Vec::with_capacity(2);\n\n                for bound_outer in [lower, upper].into_iter() {\n                    let bound = match bound_outer {\n                        types::RangeBound::Exclusive(bound)\n                        | types::RangeBound::Inclusive(bound) =&gt; bound\n                            .map(|bound| T::from_sql(&amp;inner_typ, bound))\n                            .transpose()?,\n                        types::RangeBound::Unbounded =&gt; None,\n                    };\n                    let inclusive = matches!(bound_outer, types::RangeBound::Inclusive(_));\n                    bounds.push(RangeBound { bound, inclusive });\n                }\n\n                let lower = bounds.remove(0);\n                let upper = bounds.remove(0);\n                assert!(bounds.is_empty());\n\n                Some(RangeInner {\n                    lower,\n                    // Rewrite bound in terms of appropriate &#96;UPPER&#96;\n                    upper: RangeBound {\n                        bound: upper.bound,\n                        inclusive: upper.inclusive,\n                    },\n                })\n            }\n        };\n\n        Ok(Range { inner })\n    }\n\n    fn accepts(ty: &amp;PgType) -&gt; bool {\n        matches!",
        "signatures": {},

        "contents": ",\n        })\n    }\n\n    fn parse_literal_ok(input: ParseStream) -&gt; Result {\n        let mut row = Row::default();\n        let mut packer = row.packer();\n\n        let typ = if input.eat(kw::null) {\n            packer.push(Datum::Null);\n            input.parse::&lt;syn::Token![::]&gt;()?;\n            analyses::parse_scalar_type(input)?.nullable(true)\n        } else {\n            match input.parse::&lt;syn::Lit&gt;()? {\n                syn::Lit::Str(l) =&gt; {\n                    packer.push(Datum::from(l.value().as_str()));\n                    Ok(String::as_column_type())\n                }\n                syn::Lit::Int(l) =&gt; {\n                    packer.push(Datum::from(l.base10_parse::&lt;i64&gt;()?));\n                    Ok(i64::as_column_type())\n                }\n                syn::Lit::Float(l) =&gt; {\n                    packer.push(Datum::from(l.base10_parse::&lt;f64&gt;()?));\n                    Ok(f64::as_column_type())\n                }\n                syn::Lit::Bool(l) =&gt; {\n                    packer.push(Datum::from(l.value));\n                    Ok(bool::as_column_type())\n                }\n                _ =&gt; Err(Error::new(input.span(), \"cannot parse literal\")),\n            }?\n        };\n\n        Ok(MirScalarExpr::Literal(Ok(row), typ))\n    }\n\n    fn parse_literal_err(input: ParseStream) -&gt; Result {\n        input.parse::&lt;kw::error&gt;()?;\n        let mut msg = {\n            let content;\n            syn::parenthesized!(content in input);\n            content.parse::&lt;syn::LitStr&gt;()?.value()\n        };\n        let err = if msg.starts_with(\"internal error: \") {\n            Ok(mz_expr::EvalError::Internal(msg.split_off(16).into()))\n        } else {\n            Err(Error::new(msg.span(), \"expected &#96;internal error: $msg&#96;\"))\n        }?;\n        Ok(MirScalarExpr::Literal(Err(err), bool::as_column_type())) // FIXME\n    }\n\n    fn parse_literal_array(input: ParseStream) -&gt; Result",

        "contents": "    }\n\n    fn parse_derived_table_factor(\n        &amp;mut self,\n        lateral: IsLateral,\n    ) -&gt; Result&lt;TableFactor&lt;Raw&gt;, ParserError&gt; {\n        let subquery = Box::new(self.parse_query()?);\n        self.expect_token(&amp;Token::RParen)?;\n        let alias = self.parse_optional_table_alias()?;\n        Ok(TableFactor::Derived {\n            lateral: match lateral {\n                Lateral =&gt; true,\n                NotLateral =&gt; false,\n            },\n            subquery,\n            alias,\n        })\n    }\n\n    fn parse_join_constraint(&amp;mut self, natural: bool) -&gt; Result&lt;JoinConstraint&lt;Raw&gt;, ParserError&gt; {\n        if natural {\n            Ok(JoinConstraint::Natural)\n        } else if self.parse_keyword(ON) {\n            let constraint = self.parse_expr()?;\n            Ok(JoinConstraint::On(constraint))\n        } else if self.parse_keyword(USING) {\n            let columns = self.parse_parenthesized_column_list(Mandatory)?;\n            let alias = self.parse_optional_alias(Keyword::is_reserved_in_table_alias)?;\n\n            Ok(JoinConstraint::Using { columns, alias })\n        } else {\n            self.expected(\n                self.peek_pos(),\n                \"ON, or USING after JOIN\",\n                self.peek_token(),\n            )\n        }\n    }\n\n    /// Parse an INSERT statement\n    fn parse_insert(&amp;mut self) -&gt; Result&lt;Statement&lt;Raw&gt;, ParserError&gt; {\n        self.expect_keyword(INTO)?;\n        let table_name = self.parse_raw_name()?;\n        let columns = self.parse_parenthesized_column_list(Optional)?;\n        let source = if self.parse_keywords(&amp;[DEFAULT, VALUES]) {\n            InsertSource::DefaultValues\n        } else {\n            InsertSource::Query(self.parse_query()?)\n        };\n        let returning = self.parse_returning()?;\n        Ok(Statement::Insert(InsertStatement {\n            table_name,\n            columns,\n            source,\n            returning,\n        }))\n    }\n\n    fn parse_returning(&amp;mut self) -&gt; Result&lt;Vec&lt;SelectItem&lt;Raw&gt;&gt;, ParserError&gt;",

        "contents": "macro_rules! range_fn {\n    ($fn:expr, $range_fn:expr, $sqlname:expr) =&gt; {\n        paste::paste! {\n\n            #[sqlfunc(\n                output_type = \"bool\",\n                is_infix_op = true,\n                sqlname = $sqlname,\n                propagates_nulls = true\n            )]\n            fn [&lt; range_ $fn &gt;]&lt;'a&gt;(a: Datum&lt;'a&gt;, b: Datum&lt;'a&gt;) -&gt; Datum&lt;'a&gt;\n            {\n                if a.is_null() || b.is_null() { return Datum::Null }\n                let l = a.unwrap_range();\n                let r = b.unwrap_range();\n                Datum::from(Range::&lt;Datum&lt;'a&gt;&gt;::$range_fn(&amp;l, &amp;r))\n            }\n        }\n    };\n}\n\n// RangeContainsRange is either @&gt; or &lt;@ depending on the order of the arguments.\n// It doesn't influence the result, but it does influence the display string.\nrange_fn!(contains_range, contains_range, \"@&gt;\");\nrange_fn!(contains_range_rev, contains_range, \"&lt;@\");\nrange_fn!(overlaps, overlaps, \"&amp;&amp;\");\nrange_fn!(after, after, \"&gt;&gt;\");\nrange_fn!(before, before, \"&lt;&lt;\");\nrange_fn!(overleft, overleft, \"&amp;&lt;\");\nrange_fn!(overright, overright, \"&amp;&gt;\");\nrange_fn!(adjacent, adjacent, \"-|-\");\n\n#[sqlfunc(\n    output_type_expr = \"input_type_a.scalar_type.without_modifiers().nullable(true)\",\n    is_infix_op = true,\n    sqlname = \"+\",\n    propagates_nulls = true,\n    introduces_nulls = false\n)]\nfn range_union&lt;'a&gt;(\n    l: Range&lt;Datum&lt;'a&gt;&gt;,\n    r: Range&lt;Datum&lt;'a&gt;&gt;,\n    temp_storage: &amp;'a RowArena,\n) -&gt; Result&lt;Datum&lt;'a&gt;, EvalError&gt; {\n    l.union(&amp;r)?.into_result(temp_storage)\n}\n\n#[sqlfunc(\n    output_type_expr = \"input_type_a.scalar_type.without_modifiers().nullable(true)\",\n    is_infix_op = true,\n    sqlname = \"*\",\n    propagates_nulls = true,\n    introduces_nulls = false\n)]\nfn range_intersection&lt;'a&gt;(\n    l: Range&lt;Datum&lt;'a&gt;&gt;,\n    r: Range&lt;Datum&lt;'a&gt;&gt;,\n    temp_storage: &amp;'a RowArena,\n) -&gt; Result&lt;Datum&lt;'a&gt;, EvalError&gt; {\n    l.intersection(&amp;r).into_result(temp_storage)\n}\n\n#[sqlfunc(\n    output_type_expr =",
        "signatures": {},
        "detailedLines": [
          {
            "text": "macro_rules! range_fn {",
            "lineNumber": 1499
          },
          {
            "text": "    ($fn:expr, $range_fn:expr, $sqlname:expr) =&gt; {",
            "lineNumber": 1500
          },

          {

          {

            "lineNumber": 1521

      "codeBlock": {
        "relativeWorkspacePath": "./src/repr/src/row.rs",
        "range": {
          "startPosition": {
            "line": 2537

          "endPosition": {
            "line": 2590,
            "column": 34
          }
        },
        "contents": "    ///   and &#96;upper&#96; are optional, contingent on the flag value expressing an\n    ///   empty range (where neither will be present) or infinite bounds (where\n    ///   each infinite bound will be absent).\n    /// - To push an emtpy range, use &#96;push_range&#96; using &#96;Range { inner: None }&#96;.\n    pub fn push_range_with&lt;L, U, E&gt;(\n        &amp;mut self,\n        lower: RangeLowerBound&lt;L&gt;,\n        upper: RangeUpperBound&lt;U&gt;,\n    ) -&gt; Result&lt;(), E&gt;\n    where\n        L: FnOnce(&amp;mut RowPacker) -&gt; Result&lt;(), E&gt;,\n        U: FnOnce(&amp;mut RowPacker) -&gt; Result&lt;(), E&gt;,\n        E: From&lt;InvalidRangeError&gt;,\n    {\n        let start = self.row.data.len();\n        self.row.data.push(Tag::Range.into());\n\n        let mut flags = range::InternalFlags::empty();\n\n        flags.set(range::InternalFlags::LB_INFINITE, lower.bound.is_none());\n        flags.set(range::InternalFlags::UB_INFINITE, upper.bound.is_none());\n        flags.set(range::InternalFlags::LB_INCLUSIVE, lower.inclusive);\n        flags.set(range::InternalFlags::UB_INCLUSIVE, upper.inclusive);\n\n        let mut expected_datums = 0;\n\n        self.row.data.push(flags.bits());\n\n        let datum_check = self.row.data.len();\n\n        if let Some(value) = lower.bound {\n            let start = self.row.data.len();\n            value(self)?;\n            assert!(\n                start &lt; self.row.data.len(),\n                \"finite values must each push exactly one value; expected 1 but got 0\"\n            );\n            expected_datums += 1;\n        }\n\n        if let Some(value) = upper.bound {\n            let start = self.row.data.len();\n            value(self)?;\n            assert!(\n                start &lt; self.row.data.len(),\n                \"finite values must each push exactly one value; expected 1 but got 0\"\n            );\n            expected_datums += 1;\n        }\n\n        // Validate the invariants that 0, 1, or 2 elements were pushed, none are Null,\n        // and if two are pushed then the second is not less than the first. Panic in\n        // some cases and error in others.\n        let mut actual_datums = 0;",
        "signatures": {},

          {

        "contents": "pub const TYPE_INT4_RANGE: BuiltinType&lt;NameReference&gt; = BuiltinType {\n    name: \"int4range\",\n    schema: PG_CATALOG_SCHEMA,\n    oid: mz_pgrepr::oid::TYPE_INT4RANGE_OID,\n    details: CatalogTypeDetails {\n        typ: CatalogType::Range {\n            element_reference: TYPE_INT4.name,\n        },\n        array_id: None,\n        pg_metadata: Some(CatalogTypePgMetadata {\n            typinput_oid: 3834,\n            typreceive_oid: 3836,\n        }),\n    },\n};\n\npub const TYPE_INT4_RANGE_ARRAY: BuiltinType&lt;NameReference&gt;         "contents": "    Ok(expr)\n}\n\nfn plan_literal&lt;'a&gt;(l: &amp;'a Value) -&gt; Result&lt;CoercibleScalarExpr, PlanError&gt; {\n    let (datum, scalar_type) = match l {\n        Value::Number(s) =&gt; {\n            let d = strconv::parse_numeric(s.as_str())?;\n            if !s.contains(&amp;['E', '.'][..]) {\n                // Maybe representable as an int?\n                if let Ok(n) = d.0.try_into() {\n                    (Datum::Int32(n), SqlScalarType::Int32)\n                } else if let Ok(n) = d.0.try_into() {\n                    (Datum::Int64(n), SqlScalarType::Int64)\n                } else {\n                    (\n                        Datum::Numeric(d),\n                        SqlScalarType::Numeric { max_scale: None },\n                    )\n                }\n            } else {\n                (\n                    Datum::Numeric(d),\n                    SqlScalarType::Numeric { max_scale: None },\n                )\n            }\n        }\n        Value::HexString(_) =&gt; bail_unsupported!(\"hex string literals\"),\n        Value::Boolean(b) =&gt; match b {\n            false =&gt; (Datum::False, SqlScalarType::Bool),\n            true =&gt; (Datum::True, SqlScalarType::Bool),\n        },\n        Value::Interval(i) =&gt; {\n            let i = literal::plan_interval(i)?;\n            (Datum::Interval(i), SqlScalarType::Interval)\n        }\n        Value::String(s) =&gt; return Ok(CoercibleScalarExpr::LiteralString(s.clone())),\n        Value::Null =&gt; return Ok(CoercibleScalarExpr::LiteralNull),\n    };\n    let expr = HirScalarExpr::literal(datum, scalar_type);\n    Ok(expr.into())\n}\n\n/// The common part of the planning of non-aggregate window functions, i.e.,\n/// scalar window functions and value window functions.\nfn plan_window_function_non_aggr&lt;'a&gt;(\n    ecx: &amp;ExprContext,\n    Function {\n        name,\n        args,\n        filter,\n        over,",

    {
      "codeBlock": {

          "endPosition": {
            "line": 564,
            "column": 34
          }
        },
        "contents": " Result&lt;Expr&lt;Raw&gt;, ParserError&gt; {\n        // PostgreSQL allows any string literal to be preceded by a type name,\n        // indicating that the string literal represents a literal of that type.\n        // Some examples:\n        //\n        //     DATE '2020-05-20'\n        //     TIMESTAMP WITH TIME ZONE '2020-05-20 7:43:54'\n        //     BOOL 'true'\n        //\n        // The first two are standard SQL, while the latter is a PostgreSQL\n        // extension. Complicating matters is the fact that INTERVAL string\n        // literals may optionally be followed by some special keywords, e.g.:\n        //\n        //     INTERVAL '7' DAY\n        //\n        // Note also that naively &#96;SELECT date&#96; looks like a syntax error\n        // because the &#96;date&#96; type name is not followed by a string literal, but\n        // in fact is a valid expression that should parse as the column name\n        // \"date\".\n        //\n        // Note: the maybe! block here does swallow valid parsing errors\n        // See &lt;https://github.com/MaterializeInc/incidents-and-escalations/issues/90&gt; for more details\n        maybe!(self.maybe_parse(|parser| {\n            let data_type = parser.parse_data_type()?;\n            if data_type.to_string().as_str() == \"interval\" {\n                Ok(Expr::Value(Value::Interval(parser.parse_interval_value()?)))\n            } else {\n                Ok(Expr::Cast {\n                    expr: Box::new(Expr::Value(Value::String(parser.parse_literal_string()?))),\n                    data_type,\n                })\n            }\n        }));\n\n        let tok = self\n            .next_token()\n            .ok_or_else(|| self.error(self.peek_prev_pos(), \"Unexpected EOF\".to_string()))?;\n        let expr = match (tok, self.peek_token()) {\n            (Token::LBracket, _) =&gt; {\n                self.prev_token();",

        "contents": "            Type::Range { element_type } =&gt; {\n                let range = strconv::parse_range(s, |elem_text| {\n                    Value::decode_text(element_type, elem_text.as_bytes()).map(Box::new)\n                })?;\n                // TODO: We should be able to push ranges without scratch space, but that requires\n                // a different &#96;push_range&#96; API.\n                let buf = RowArena::new();\n                let range = range.into_bounds(|elem| elem.into_datum(&amp;buf, element_type));\n\n                packer.push_range(range).unwrap()\n            }\n            Type::MzAclItem =&gt; packer.push(Datum::MzAclItem(strconv::parse_mz_acl_item(s)?)),\n            Type::AclItem =&gt; packer.push(Datum::AclItem(strconv::parse_acl_item(s)?)),\n        })\n    }\n\n    /// Deserializes a value of type &#96;ty&#96; from &#96;raw&#96; using the [binary encoding\n    /// format](Format::Binary).\n    pub fn decode_binary(ty: &amp;Type, raw: &amp;[u8]) -&gt; Result&lt;Value, Box&lt;dyn Error + Sync + Send&gt;&gt; {\n        match ty {\n            Type::Array(_) =&gt;",

        "contents": " {\n        match self.next_token() {\n            Some(t) =&gt; match t {\n                Token::Keyword(TRUE) =&gt; Ok(Value::Boolean(true)),\n                Token::Keyword(FALSE) =&gt; Ok(Value::Boolean(false)),\n                Token::Keyword(NULL) =&gt; Ok(Value::Null),\n                Token::Keyword(INTERVAL) =&gt; Ok(Value::Interval(self.parse_interval_value()?)),\n                Token::Keyword(kw) =&gt; {\n                    parser_err!(\n                        self,\n                        self.peek_prev_pos(),\n                        format!(\"No value parser for keyword {}\", kw)\n                    )\n                }\n                Token::Op(ref op) if op == \"-\" =&gt; match self.next_token() {\n                    Some(Token::Number(n)) =&gt; Ok(Value::Number(format!(\"-{}\", n))),\n                    other =&gt; self.expected(self.peek_prev_pos(), \"literal int\", other),\n                },\n                Token::Number(ref n) =&gt; Ok(Value::Number(n.to_string())),\n                Token::String(ref s) =&gt; Ok(Value::String(s.to_string())),\n                Token::HexString(ref s) =&gt; Ok(Value::HexString(s.to_string())),\n                _ =&gt; parser_err!(\n                    self,\n                    self.peek_prev_pos(),\n                    format!(\"Unsupported value: {:?}\", t)\n                ),\n            },\n            None =&gt; parser_err!(\n                self,\n                self.peek_prev_pos(),\n                \"Expecting a value, but found EOF\"\n            ),\n        }\n    }\n\n    fn parse_array(&amp;mut self) -&gt; Result&lt;Expr&lt;Raw&gt;, ParserError&gt; {\n        if self.consume_token(&amp;Token::LParen) {\n            let subquery = self.parse_query()?;\n            self.expect_token(&amp;Token::RParen)?;\n            Ok(Expr::ArraySubquery(Box::new(subquery)))\n        } else {\n            self.parse_sequence(Self::parse_array).map(Expr::Array)\n        }\n    }\n\n    fn parse_list(&amp;mut self) -&gt; Result&lt;Expr&lt;Raw&gt;, ParserError&gt;",

        "contents": "impl LazyUnaryFunc for CastStringToRange {\n    fn eval&lt;'a&gt;(\n        &amp;'a self,\n        datums: &amp;[Datum&lt;'a&gt;],\n        temp_storage: &amp;'a RowArena,\n        a: &amp;'a MirScalarExpr,\n    ) -&gt; Result&lt;Datum&lt;'a&gt;, EvalError&gt; {\n        let a = a.eval(datums, temp_storage)?;\n        if a.is_null() {\n            return Ok(Datum::Null);\n        }\n        let mut range = strconv::parse_range(a.unwrap_str(), |elem_text| {\n            let elem_text = match elem_text {\n                Cow::Owned(s) =&gt; temp_storage.push_string(s),\n                Cow::Borrowed(s) =&gt; s,\n            };\n            self.cast_expr\n                .eval(&amp;[Datum::String(elem_text)], temp_storage)\n        })?;\n\n        range.canonicalize()?;\n\n        Ok(temp_storage.make_datum(|packer| {\n            packer\n                .push_range(range)\n                .expect(\"must have already handled errors\")\n        }))\n    }\n\n    /// The output SqlColumnType of this function\n    fn output_type(&amp;self, input_type: SqlColumnType) -&gt; SqlColumnType {\n        self.return_ty\n            .without_modifiers()\n            .nullable(input_type.nullable)\n    }\n\n    /// Whether this function will produce NULL on NULL input\n    fn propagates_nulls(&amp;self) -&gt; bool {\n        true\n    }\n\n    /// Whether this function will produce NULL on non-NULL input\n    fn introduces_nulls(&amp;self) -&gt; bool {\n        false\n    }\n\n    /// Whether this function preserves uniqueness\n    fn preserves_uniqueness(&amp;self) -&gt; bool {\n        false\n    }\n\n    fn inverse(&amp;self) -&gt; Option&lt;crate::UnaryFunc&gt; {\n        to_unary!(super::CastRangeToString {\n            ty: self.return_ty.clone(),\n        })\n    }\n\n    fn is_monotone(&amp;self) -&gt; bool {\n        false\n    }\n}\n\nimpl fmt::Display for CastStringToRange {\n    fn fmt(&amp;self, f: &amp;mut fmt::Formatter) -&gt; fmt::Result {\n        f.write_str(\"strtorange\")\n    }\n}\n\n#[derive(\n    Ord,\n    PartialOrd,\n    Clone,\n    Debug,\n    Eq,\n    PartialEq,\n    Serialize,\n    Deserialize,\n    Hash,\n    MzReflect\n)]\npub struct CastStringToVarChar {\n    pub length: Option&lt;VarCharMaxLength&gt;,",

      "codeBlock": {

        "contents": "mod row {\n    use mz_repr::{Datum, Row, RowPacker};\n\n    use super::*;\n\n    impl Parse for Parsed&lt;Row&gt; {\n        fn parse(input: ParseStream) -&gt; syn::Result&lt;Self&gt; {\n            let mut row = Row::default();\n            let mut packer = ParseRow::new(&amp;mut row);\n\n            loop {\n                if input.is_empty() {\n                    break;\n                }\n                packer.parse_datum(input)?;\n                if input.is_empty() {\n                    break;\n                }\n                input.parse::&lt;syn::Token![,]&gt;()?;\n            }\n\n            Ok(Parsed(row))\n        }\n    }\n\n    impl From&lt;Parsed&lt;Row&gt;&gt; for Row {\n        fn from(parsed: Parsed&lt;Row&gt;) -&gt; Self {\n            parsed.0\n        }\n    }\n\n    struct ParseRow&lt;'a&gt;(RowPacker&lt;'a&gt;);\n\n    impl&lt;'a&gt; ParseRow&lt;'a&gt; {\n        fn new(row: &amp;'a mut Row) -&gt; Self {\n            Self(row.packer())\n        }\n\n        fn parse_datum(&amp;mut self, input: ParseStream) -&gt; syn::Result&lt;()&gt; {\n            if input.eat(kw::null) {\n                self.0.push(Datum::Null)\n            } else {\n                match input.parse::&lt;syn::Lit&gt;()? {\n                    syn::Lit::Str(l) =&gt; self.0.push(Datum::from(l.value().as_str())),\n                    syn::Lit::Int(l) =&gt; self.0.push(Datum::from(l.base10_parse::&lt;i64&gt;()?)),\n                    syn::Lit::Float(l) =&gt; self.0.push(Datum::from(l.base10_parse::&lt;f64&gt;()?)),\n                    syn::Lit::Bool(l) =&gt; self.0.push(Datum::from(l.value)),\n                    _ =&gt; Err(Error::new(input.span(), \"cannot parse literal\"))?,\n                }\n            }\n            Ok(())\n        }\n    }\n}\n\nmod analyses {\n    use mz_repr::{SqlColumnType, SqlScalarType};\n\n    use super::*;",

        "contents": "// Copyright Materialize, Inc. and contributors. All rights reserved.\n//\n// Use of this software is governed by the Business Source License\n// included in the LICENSE file.\n//\n// As of the Change Date specified in that file, in accordance with\n// the Business Source License, use of this software will be governed\n// by the Apache License, Version 2.0.\n\nuse std::any::type_name;\nuse std::cmp::Ordering;\nuse std::error::Error;\nuse std::fmt::{self, Debug, Display};\nuse std::hash::{Hash, Hasher};\n\nuse bitflags::bitflags;\nuse chrono::{DateTime, NaiveDateTime, Utc};\nuse dec::OrderedDecimal;\nuse mz_lowertest::MzReflect;\nuse mz_proto::{RustType, TryFromProtoError};\nuse postgres_protocol::types;\nuse proptest_derive::Arbitrary;\nuse serde::{Deserialize, Serialize};\nuse tokio_postgres::types::{FromSql, Type as PgType};\n\nuse crate::Datum;\nuse crate::adt::date::Date;\nuse crate::adt::numeric::Numeric;\nuse crate::adt::timestamp::CheckedTimestamp;\nuse crate::scalar::DatumKind;\n\ninclude!(concat!(env!(\"OUT_DIR\"), \"/mz_repr.adt.range.rs\"));\n\nbitflags! {\n    pub(crate) struct InternalFlags: u8 {\n        const EMPTY = 1;\n        const LB_INCLUSIVE = 1 &lt;&lt; 1;\n        const LB_INFINITE = 1 &lt;&lt; 2;\n        const UB_INCLUSIVE = 1 &lt;&lt; 3;\n        const UB_INFINITE = 1 &lt;&lt; 4;\n    }\n}\n\nbitflags! {\n    pub(crate) struct PgFlags: u8 {\n        const EMPTY = 0b0000_0001;\n        const LB_INCLUSIVE = 0b0000_0010;\n        const UB_INCLUSIVE = 0b0000_0100;\n        const LB_INFINITE = 0b0000_1000;\n        const UB_INFINITE = 0b0001_0000;\n    }\n}\n\n/// A range of values along the domain &#96;D&#96;.\n///\n/// &#96;D&#96; is generic to facilitate interoperating over multiple representation,\n/// e.g. &#96;Datum&#96; and &#96;mz_pgrepr::Value&#96;. Because of the latter, we have to\n/// \"manually derive\" traits over &#96;Range&#96;.\n///\n/// Also notable, is that &#96;Datum&#96;s themselves store ranges as\n/// &#96;Range&lt;DatumNested&lt;'a&gt;&gt;&#96;, which lets us avoid unnecessary boxing of the\n/// range's finite bounds, which are most often expressed as &#96;Datum&#96;.\npub struct Range&lt;D&gt; {\n    /// None value represents empty range",

    {
      "codeBlock": {
        "relativeWorkspacePath": "./src/expr-parser/src/parser.rs",
        "range": {
          "startPosition": {
            "line": 120,
            "column": 5
          },
          "endPosition": {
            "line": 171,
            "column": 59
          }
        },
        "contents": "    fn parse_constant(ctx: CtxRef, input: ParseStream) -&gt; Result {\n        let constant = input.parse::&lt;kw::Constant&gt;()?;\n\n        let parse_typ = |input: ParseStream| -&gt; syn::Result&lt;SqlRelationType&gt; {\n            let analyses = analyses::parse_analyses(input)?;\n            let Some(column_types) = analyses.types else {\n                let msg = \"Missing expected &#96;types&#96; analyses for Constant line\";\n                Err(Error::new(input.span(), msg))?\n            };\n            let keys = analyses.keys.unwrap_or_default();\n            Ok(SqlRelationType { column_types, keys })\n        };\n\n        if input.eat3(syn::Token![&lt;], kw::empty, syn::Token![&gt;]) {\n            let typ = parse_typ(input)?;\n            Ok(MirRelationExpr::constant(vec![], typ))\n        } else {\n            let typ = parse_typ(input)?;\n            let parse_children = ParseChildren::new(input, constant.span().start());\n            let rows = Ok(parse_children.parse_many(ctx, parse_constant_entry)?);\n            Ok(MirRelationExpr::Constant { rows, typ })\n        }\n    }\n\n    fn parse_constant_entry(_ctx: CtxRef, input: ParseStream) -&gt; syn::Result&lt;(Row, Diff)&gt; {\n        input.parse::&lt;syn::Token![-]&gt;()?;\n\n        let (row, diff);\n\n        let inner1;\n        syn::parenthesized!(inner1 in input);\n\n        if inner1.peek(syn::token::Paren) {\n            let inner2;\n            syn::parenthesized!(inner2 in inner1);\n            row = inner2.parse::&lt;Parsed&lt;Row&gt;&gt;()?.into();\n            inner1.parse::&lt;kw::x&gt;()?;\n            diff = match inner1.parse::&lt;syn::Lit&gt;()? {\n                syn::Lit::Int(l) =&gt; Ok(l.base10_parse::&lt;Diff&gt;()?),\n                _ =&gt; Err(Error::new(inner1.span(), \"expected Diff literal\")),\n            }?;\n        } else {\n            row = inner1.parse::&lt;Parsed&lt;Row&gt;&gt;()?.into();\n            diff = Diff::ONE;\n        }\n\n        Ok((row, diff))\n    }\n\n    fn parse_get(ctx: CtxRef, input: ParseStream) -&gt; Result",
        "signatures": {},

        "contents": " {\n        let negative = self.consume_token(&amp;Token::Op(\"-\".into()));\n        match self.next_token() {\n            Some(Token::Number(s)) =&gt; {\n                let n = s.parse::&lt;i64&gt;().map_err(|e| {\n                    self.error(\n                        self.peek_prev_pos(),\n                        format!(\"Could not parse '{}' as i64: {}\", s, e),\n                    )\n                })?;\n                if negative {\n                    n.checked_neg().ok_or_else(|| {\n                        self.error(\n                            self.peek_prev_pos(),\n                            format!(\"Could not parse '{}' as i64: overflows i64\", s),\n                        )\n                    })\n                } else {\n                    Ok(n)\n                }\n            }\n            other =&gt; self.expected(self.peek_prev_pos(), \"literal integer\", other),\n        }\n    }\n\n    /// Parse an unsigned literal integer.\n    fn parse_literal_uint(&amp;mut self) -&gt; Result&lt;u64, ParserError&gt; {\n        match self.next_token() {\n            Some(Token::Number(s)) =&gt; s.parse::&lt;u64&gt;().map_err(|e| {\n                self.error(\n                    self.peek_prev_pos(),\n                    format!(\"Could not parse '{}' as u64: {}\", s, e),\n                )\n            }),\n            other =&gt; self.expected(self.peek_prev_pos(), \"literal unsigned integer\", other),\n        }\n    }\n\n    /// Parse a literal string\n    fn parse_literal_string(&amp;mut self) -&gt; Result&lt;String, ParserError&gt; {\n        match self.next_token() {\n            Some(Token::String(ref s)) =&gt; Ok(s.clone()),\n            other =&gt; self.expected(self.peek_prev_pos(), \"literal string\", other),\n        }\n    }\n\n    /// Parse a SQL datatype (in the context of a CREATE TABLE statement for example)\n    fn parse_data_type(&amp;mut self) -&gt; Result&lt;RawDataType, ParserError&gt; {\n        let other = |ident| RawDataType::Other {\n            name: RawItemName::Name(UnresolvedItemName::unqualified(ident)),",

        "contents": "    fn accepts(ty: &amp;PgType) -&gt; bool {\n        matches!(\n            ty,\n            &amp;PgType::INT4_RANGE\n                | &amp;PgType::INT8_RANGE\n                | &amp;PgType::DATE_RANGE\n                | &amp;PgType::NUM_RANGE\n                | &amp;PgType::TS_RANGE\n                | &amp;PgType::TSTZ_RANGE\n        )\n    }\n}",

        "contents": "    let expr = match source {\n        InsertSource::Query(mut query) =&gt; {\n            transform_ast::transform(scx, &amp;mut query)?;\n\n            match query {\n                // Special-case simple VALUES clauses as PostgreSQL does.\n                Query {\n                    body: SetExpr::Values(Values(values)),\n                    ctes,\n                    order_by,\n                    limit: None,\n                    offset: None,\n                } if ctes.is_empty() &amp;&amp; order_by.is_empty() =&gt; {\n                    let names: Vec&lt;_&gt; = ordering.iter().map(|i| desc.get_name(*i)).collect();\n                    plan_values_insert(&amp;qcx, &amp;names, &amp;source_types, &amp;values)?\n                }\n                _ =&gt; {\n                    let (expr, _scope) = plan_nested_query(&amp;mut qcx, &amp;query)?;\n                    expr\n                }\n            }\n        }\n        InsertSource::DefaultValues =&gt; {\n            HirRelationExpr::constant(vec![vec![]], SqlRelationType::empty())\n        }\n    };\n\n    let expr_arity = expr.arity();\n\n    // Validate that the arity of the source query is at most the size of declared columns or the\n    // size of the table if none are declared\n    let max_columns = if columns.is_empty() {\n        desc.arity()\n    } else {\n        columns.len()\n    };\n    if expr_arity &gt; max_columns {\n        sql_bail!(\"INSERT has more expressions than target columns\");\n    }\n    // But it should never have less than the declared columns (or zero)\n    if expr_arity &lt; columns.len() {\n        sql_bail!(\"INSERT has more target columns than expressions\");\n    }\n\n    // Trim now that we know for sure the correct arity of the source query\n    source_types.truncate(expr_arity);\n    ordering.truncate(expr_arity);\n\n    // Ensure the types of the source query match the types of the target table,\n    // installing assignment casts where necessary and possible.\n    let expr = cast_relation(&amp;qcx, CastContext::Assignment, expr, source_types).map_err(|e| {\n        sql_err!(\n            \"",

        "contents": "where\n    I: Iterator&lt;Item = (&amp;'a str, &amp;'a SqlScalarType)&gt;,\n{\n    let temp_storage = RowArena::new();\n    Row::try_pack(datum_iter.map(|(litval, littyp)| {\n        if litval == \"null\" {\n            Ok(Datum::Null)\n        } else {\n            match littyp {\n                SqlScalarType::Bool =&gt; Ok(Datum::from(parse_litval::&lt;bool&gt;(litval, \"bool\")?)),\n                SqlScalarType::Numeric { .. } =&gt; {\n                    Ok(Datum::from(parse_litval::&lt;Numeric&gt;(litval, \"Numeric\")?))\n                }\n                SqlScalarType::Int16 =&gt; Ok(Datum::from(parse_litval::&lt;i16&gt;(litval, \"i16\")?)),\n                SqlScalarType::Int32 =&gt; Ok(Datum::from(parse_litval::&lt;i32&gt;(litval, \"i32\")?)),\n                SqlScalarType::Int64 =&gt; Ok(Datum::from(parse_litval::&lt;i64&gt;(litval, \"i64\")?)),\n                SqlScalarType::Float32 =&gt; Ok(Datum::from(parse_litval::&lt;f32&gt;(litval, \"f32\")?)),\n                SqlScalarType::Float64 =&gt; Ok(Datum::from(parse_litval::&lt;f64&gt;(litval, \"f64\")?)),\n                SqlScalarType::String =&gt; Ok(Datum::from(\n                    temp_storage.push_string(mz_lowertest::unquote(litval)),\n                )),\n                SqlScalarType::Timestamp { .. } =&gt; {\n                    let datetime = if litval.contains('.') {\n                        NaiveDateTime::parse_from_str(litval, \"\\\"%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S%.f\\\"\")\n                    } else {\n                        NaiveDateTime::parse_from_str(litval, \"\\\"%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S\\\"\")\n                    };\n                    Ok(Datum::from(\n                        CheckedTimestamp::from_timestamplike(\n                            datetime\n                                .map_err(|e| format!(\"Error while parsing NaiveDateTime: {}\", e))?,\n                        )\n                        .unwrap(),\n                    ))\n                }\n                SqlScalarType::Jsonb =&gt; parse_jsonb(&amp;mz_lowertest::unquote(litval))\n                    .map",

        "contents": " Result&lt;(), InvalidRangeError&gt; {\n        range.canonicalize()?;\n        match range.inner {\n            None =&gt; {\n                self.row.data.push(Tag::Range.into());\n                // Untagged bytes only contains the &#96;RANGE_EMPTY&#96; flag value.\n                self.row.data.push(range::InternalFlags::EMPTY.bits());\n                Ok(())\n            }\n            Some(inner) =&gt; self.push_range_with(\n                RangeLowerBound {\n                    inclusive: inner.lower.inclusive,\n                    bound: inner\n                        .lower\n                        .bound\n                        .map(|value| move |row: &amp;mut RowPacker| Ok(row.push(value))),\n                },\n                RangeUpperBound {\n                    inclusive: inner.upper.inclusive,\n                    bound: inner\n                        .upper\n                        .bound\n                        .map(|value| move |row: &amp;mut RowPacker| Ok(row.push(value))),\n                },\n            ),\n        }\n    }\n\n    /// Pushes a &#96;DatumRange&#96; built from the specified arguments.\n    ///\n    /// # Warning\n    /// Unlike &#96;push_range&#96;, &#96;push_range_with&#96; _does not_ canonicalize its\n    /// inputs. Consequentially, this means it's possible to generate ranges\n    /// that will not reflect the proper ordering and equality.\n    ///\n    /// # Panics\n    /// - If lower or upper expresses a finite value and does not push exactly\n    ///   one value into the &#96;RowPacker&#96;.\n    /// - If lower and upper express finite values and they are datums of\n    ///   different types.\n    /// - If lower or upper express finite values and push &#96;Datum::Null&#96;.\n    ///\n    /// # Notes\n    /// - Prefer &#96;push_range_with&#96; over this function. This function should be\n    ///   used only when you are not pushing &#96;Datum&#96;s to the inner row.\n    /// - Range encoding is &#96;[&lt;flag bytes&gt;,&lt;lower&gt;?,&lt;upper&gt;?]&#96;, where &#96;lower&#96;\n    ///   and &#96;upper&#96; are optional, contingent on the flag value expressing an\n    ///   empty range (where neither will be present) or infinite bounds (where",

          }
        },
        "contents": "impl&lt;D&gt; Range&lt;D&gt; {\n    /// Create a new range.\n    ///\n    /// Note that when constructing &#96;Range&lt;Datum&lt;'a&gt;&gt;&#96;, the range must still be\n    /// canonicalized. If this becomes a common operation, we should consider\n    /// addinga &#96;new_canonical&#96; function that performs both steps.\n    pub fn new(inner: Option&lt;(RangeLowerBound&lt;D&gt;, RangeUpperBound&lt;D&gt;)&gt;) -&gt; Range&lt;D&gt; {\n        Range {\n            inner: inner.map(|(lower, upper)| RangeInner { lower, upper }),\n        }\n    }\n\n    /// Get the flag bits appropriate to use in our internal (i.e. row) encoding\n    /// of range values.\n    ///\n    /// Note that this differs from the flags appropriate to encode with\n    /// Postgres, which has &#96;UB_INFINITE&#96; and &#96;LB_INCLUSIVE&#96; in the alternate\n    /// position.\n    pub fn internal_flag_bits(&amp;self) -&gt; u8 {\n        let mut flags = InternalFlags::empty();\n\n        match &amp;self.inner {\n            None =&gt; {\n                flags.set(InternalFlags::EMPTY, true);\n            }\n            Some(RangeInner { lower, upper }) =&gt; {\n                flags.set(InternalFlags::EMPTY, false);\n                flags.set(InternalFlags::LB_INFINITE, lower.bound.is_none());\n                flags.set(InternalFlags::UB_INFINITE, upper.bound.is_none());\n                flags.set(InternalFlags::LB_INCLUSIVE, lower.inclusive);\n                flags.set(InternalFlags::UB_INCLUSIVE, upper.inclusive);\n            }\n        }\n\n        flags.bits()\n    }\n\n    /// Get the flag bits appropriate to use in PG-compatible encodings of range\n    /// values.\n    ///\n    /// Note that this differs from the flags appropriate for our internal\n    /// encoding, which has &#96;UB_INFINITE&#96; and &#96;LB_INCLUSIVE&#96; in the alternate\n    /// position.\n    pub fn pg_flag_bits(&amp;self) -&gt; u8 {\n        let mut flags = PgFlags::empty();\n\n        match &amp;self.inner {\n            None =&gt;",

        "contents": " {\n        let s = str::from_utf8(raw)?;\n        Ok(match ty {\n            Type::Array(elem_type) =&gt; {\n                let (elements, dims) = strconv::parse_array(\n                    s,\n                    || None,\n                    |elem_text| Value::decode_text(elem_type, elem_text.as_bytes()).map(Some),\n                )?;\n                Value::Array { dims, elements }\n            }\n            Type::Int2Vector { .. } =&gt; {\n                return Err(\"input of Int2Vector types is not implemented\".into());\n            }\n            Type::Bool =&gt; Value::Bool(strconv::parse_bool(s)?),\n            Type::Bytea =&gt; Value::Bytea(strconv::parse_bytes(s)?),\n            Type::Char =&gt; Value::Char(raw.get(0).copied().unwrap_or(0)),\n            Type::Date =&gt; Value::Date(strconv::parse_date(s)?),\n            Type::Float4 =&gt; Value::Float4(strconv::parse_float32(s)?),\n            Type::Float8 =&gt; Value::Float8(strconv::parse_float64(s)?),\n            Type::Int2 =&gt; Value::Int2(strconv::parse_int16(s)?),\n            Type::Int4 =&gt;         "contents": " Numeric, 1710;\n        },\n        \"tsrange\" =&gt; Scalar {\n            params!(Timestamp, Timestamp) =&gt; Operation::variadic(|_ecx, mut exprs| {\n                exprs.push(HirScalarExpr::literal(\n                    Datum::String(\"[)\"), SqlScalarType::String,\n                ));\n                Ok(HirScalarExpr::call_variadic(\n                    VariadicFunc::RangeCreate {\n                        elem_type: SqlScalarType::Timestamp { precision: None },\n                    },\n                    exprs,\n                ))\n            }) =&gt; SqlScalarType::Range {\n                element_type: Box::new(SqlScalarType::Timestamp { precision: None }),\n            }, 3933;\n            params!(Timestamp, Timestamp, String) =&gt; Operation::variadic(|_ecx, exprs| {\n                Ok(HirScalarExpr::call_variadic(\n                    VariadicFunc::RangeCreate {\n                        elem_type: SqlScalarType::Timestamp { precision: None },\n                    },\n                    exprs,\n                ))\n            }) =&gt; SqlScalarType::Range {\n                element_type: Box::new(SqlScalarType::Timestamp { precision: None }),\n            }, 3934;\n        },\n        \"tstzrange\" =&gt; Scalar {\n            params!(TimestampTz, TimestampTz) =&gt; Operation::variadic(|_ecx, mut exprs| {\n                exprs.push(HirScalarExpr::literal",

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

The range.slt tests show exactly how to insert. You can use either:

1. **String literal with explicit cast**: `'[7,10)'::int4range`
2. **int4range constructor**: `int4range(7, 10)` (and variants like `int4range(7, 10, '[]')` for bounds)

From the test file:
- `INSERT INTO int4range_values VALUES ('[,1)'::int4range), ...`
- `INSERT INTO int4range_test_values VALUES ('empty'), ('(,)'), ...` (when the column type is known, the string can be used and cast implicitly in some cases)

So for the user's table `create table t(r int4range)`, they can do:

```sql
INSERT INTO t VALUES ('[7,10)'::int4range);
```

Or with the constructor:
```sql
INSERT INTO t VALUES (int4range(7, 10));
```

The `[7,10)` means: inclusive lower bound 7, exclusive upper bound 10. The default for `int4range(low, high)` is `[)` (inclusive lower, exclusive upper), so `int4range(7, 10)` is equivalent to `'[7,10)'::int4range`.

Let me give a clear answer.
</details></think>

---


You can insert into `t` in two main ways:

**1. String literal with cast (same style as COPY)**

```sql
INSERT INTO t VALUES ('[7,10)'::int4range);
```

**2. Constructor**

```sql
INSERT INTO t VALUES (int4range(7, 10));
```

`int4range(7, 10)` is the default `[lower, upper)` (inclusive lower, exclusive upper), so it matches `[7,10)`.

Other examples from the tests:

- **Unbounded:** `'[,)'::int4range`, `'(,]'::int4range`, `'(,)'::int4range`
- **Empty:** `'empty'::int4range`
- **Custom bounds with constructor:** `int4range(7, 10, '[]')` for `[7,10]`, `int4range(7, 10, '()')` for `(7,10)`, etc.

So for your case, either is fine:

```sql
INSERT INTO t VALUES ('[7,10)'::int4range);
-- or
INSERT INTO t VALUES (int4range(7, 10));
```

---

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

The user wants to understand why COPY panics on an invalid range like `[7, 3)` while INSERT returns a proper error. They've pointed to the panic at `src/pgrepr/src/value.rs:313:66` with `MisorderedRangeBounds`. They don't want code changes—just an explanation of the difference.

Let me trace through:

1. **INSERT path**: When you do `INSERT INTO t VALUES (int4range(7, 3))`, the range is constructed and validated somewhere in the SQL/planning path—likely when the `int4range()` function is evaluated or when the range type is validated. That path returns a proper SQL error.

2. **COPY path**: COPY reads raw text and decodes it. The stack trace shows:
   - `Value::into_datum` in pgrepr/value.rs:313
   - called from `decode_copy_format` in mz_pgcopy
   - So COPY parses the text into a `Value` (probably a range value), then converts that `Value` into a `Datum` via `into_datum`. The panic is in that conversion—when creating the datum from the range value, it's unwrapping a Result that contains `MisorderedRangeBounds`.

So the difference is:
- **INSERT**: The range is built through the SQL expression path. The `int4range()` constructor (or the range literal parsing) validates that lower ≤ upper and returns an error before we get to inserting.
- **COPY**: The text is parsed into a `Value` (pgrepr layer). That parsing might not validate the range bounds ordering—or it might produce a Range that allows misordered bounds. Then when converting `Value` to `Datum` for Materialize's internal representation, the code unwraps and panics on `MisorderedRangeBounds` instead of propagating the error.

Let me look at the code to confirm this understanding.
</details></think>

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

So the panic is at line 314: `buf.make_datum(|packer| packer.push_range(range).unwrap())`. The `push_range` returns a `Result` that can be `Err(InvalidRangeError::MisorderedRangeBounds)`, but the code calls `.unwrap()` instead of propagating the error. So when COPY produces a range with misordered bounds, we get to this point and panic.

Now: where does the INSERT path validate? The range gets built via the SQL expression path. Let me find where that validation happens—likely in the range constructor or when parsing a range literal. The INSERT path would go through typeconv/planning and then evaluation. When you call `int4range(7, 3)`, the range is constructed and at some point the invariant (lower <= upper) is checked and an error is returned. That's probably in `Range::new` or when pushing to the row in a way that returns the error.

Let me check where the INSERT path validates—likely in repr/adt/range.rs when constructing, and in row.rs push_range. So:
- INSERT: expression evaluates to a Range; when that range is eventually written (or when the range is constructed), the validation happens and returns Err, which gets turned into a user-facing error.
- COPY: text is decoded into pgrepr::Value::Range. The pgrepr Range type might allow misordered bounds (it's a different representation). Then Value::into_datum calls push_range, which returns Err(MisorderedRangeBounds), but into_datum uses .unwrap() so it panics.

So the "why" is: COPY path uses .unwrap() on the result of push_range in value.rs, instead of propagating the error to the user. The INSERT path propagates the error because the range is constructed and validated in a context that returns Result and that error is turned into a protocol error.
</details></think>

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

Now I see the full picture.

**INSERT path:**
1. Expression `int4range(7, 3)` is evaluated.
2. That builds a `Range` via the repr layer (e.g. in range.rs). The constructor/validation in `Range` (e.g. the code at 497-498) checks `if l > u { return Err(InvalidRangeError::MisorderedRangeBounds) }`. So the error is returned as a Result and eventually surfaces as a user-visible SQL error.

**COPY path:**
1. Text `[7, 3)` is decoded. In value.rs line 676 we see: `Value::Range(strconv::parse_range(s, ...)?)`. So `strconv::parse_range` parses the string and produces a range *without* validating that lower <= upper. The pgrepr `Range` type (or the parsed representation) can represent misordered bounds.
2. Later, in COPY, we call `Value::into_datum`. For `Value::Range(range)` (lines 307-314), we do `range.into_bounds(...)` to get a repr `Range`, then `packer.push_range(range).unwrap()`. The `push_range` in row.rs validates and returns `Err(MisorderedRangeBounds)` when `seen > d`, but the pgrepr code uses `.unwrap()`, so we panic instead of returning the error to the user.

So the difference is:
- **INSERT**: Validation happens when the range is *constructed* in the SQL/expr path (repr::adt::range), and the error is returned and turned into a protocol error.
- **COPY**: Parsing (strconv::parse_range) builds a range value that can have misordered bounds; validation only happens when we convert to Datum via `push_range`. That conversion correctly returns `Err(MisorderedRangeBounds)`, but the caller in pgrepr/value.rs uses `.unwrap()`, so the error becomes a panic instead of a user-visible error.

I'll summarize this for the user without making code changes.
</details></think>

---

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

So `into_datum` returns `Datum<'a>`, not `Result<Datum<'a>, _>`. So the design is that conversion from Value to Datum is assumed to be infallible. But for ranges, `push_range` can fail with `MisorderedRangeBounds`. So the bug is that the pgrepr layer doesn't have a way to surface that error—it just unwraps. So the difference is:

1. **INSERT**: The range is built and validated in the repr/expr path. The `int4range()` function (or range literal) creates a Range, and that creation path validates lower <= upper and returns `Err(InvalidRangeError::MisorderedRangeBounds)`. That error propagates up and becomes a user-visible SQL error.

2. **COPY**: The text is decoded by `Value::decode_text()` which uses `strconv::parse_range()`. That parser just parses the syntax (brackets, numbers, etc.) and builds a pgrepr Range value. It doesn't check that lower <= upper—so we successfully get a `Value::Range` with misordered bounds. Then when we call `value.into_datum()`, internally it calls `packer.push_range(range).unwrap()`. The `push_range` in row.rs does check and returns Err(MisorderedRangeBounds), but into_datum has no way to return that—it returns Datum, not Result—so the code unwraps and panics.

So the summary for the user:
- **INSERT**: Validation happens at range construction time (in the SQL/expr/repr path). The constructor returns Result and the error is propagated as a normal SQL error.
- **COPY**: (1) Parsing only checks syntax, not semantics, so misordered bounds are accepted as a valid Value. (2) Validation happens only when converting Value → Datum (push_range). That conversion correctly returns Err, but into_datum is not designed to return errors—it returns Datum and uses .unwrap(), so the error becomes a panic.
</details></think>

---


Here’s what’s going on.

---

## Why INSERT errors and COPY panics

**INSERT path**

1. The expression `int4range(7, 3)` is evaluated in the SQL/expr path.
2. The range is built in the repr layer (`mz_repr::adt::range`). There, the constructor validates bounds and returns a `Result`; if lower > upper it does:
   - `return Err(InvalidRangeError::MisorderedRangeBounds)`
3. That error is propagated and turned into a normal SQL error, so you see:  
   `ERROR: range lower bound must be less than or equal to range upper bound`.

**COPY path**

1. The text `[7, 3)` is decoded with `Value::decode_text()` → `strconv::parse_range()`. The parser only cares about syntax (brackets, numbers, commas). It does **not** check that lower ≤ upper, so it successfully produces a `Value::Range` with bounds 7 and 3.
2. COPY then converts that value to Materialize’s internal form by calling `value.into_datum(&buf, typ)` (in `pgcopy`).
3. For `Value::Range`, `into_datum` (in `pgrepr/src/value.rs` around 307–314) does:
   - build the repr `Range` from the pgrepr range,
   - then `packer.push_range(range).unwrap()`.
4. `push_range` in `repr/src/row.rs` (around 2607–2610) **does** validate: when it sees the second bound is less than the first it returns `Err(InvalidRangeError::MisorderedRangeBounds)`.
5. But `into_datum` is defined to return `Datum<'a>`, not `Result<..., _>`. So the only way the COPY path can use `push_range` there is to `.unwrap()`. When the error occurs, that turns into a panic instead of a user-visible error.

So the **difference** is:

- **INSERT**: The range is created and validated in code that returns `Result`. The “lower ≤ upper” check happens there and the error is returned and shown as a normal SQL error.
- **COPY**: Parsing accepts misordered bounds as a valid `Value`. The same validation only happens later, when converting to `Datum` via `push_range`. That conversion can fail, but the caller (`into_datum`) has no way to return that failure (it returns `Datum`), so it uses `.unwrap()` and you get a panic instead of an error message.

In short: COPY never checks range ordering during parsing, and the only place that does check it (when building the `Datum`) is in a function that doesn’t propagate errors, so invalid ranges from COPY cause a panic instead of a proper error.

---

_**User**_

Ok, so how should we think about fixing this. One suggestion is:

just make into_datum return a Result, so that it doesn't need to unwrap, and handle the result appropriately at every call.

Alternatively we somehow check in the copy stage

---

_**Agent (model default, mode Agent)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

The user is asking for advice on how to fix the COPY panic for invalid ranges. They've outlined two approaches:

1. Make `into_datum` return a `Result` so it doesn't need to unwrap, and handle the result at every call site.
2. Validate the range earlier, in the COPY stage (e.g., when decoding or before calling into_datum).

Let me think through the tradeoffs:

**Option 1: into_datum returns Result**
- Pros: Fixes the root cause—any conversion that can fail can now propagate the error. The range validation stays in one place (push_range).
- Cons: `into_datum` has many call sites. I need to check how many and what the impact is. Changing the signature to return Result means every caller must handle it. Some callers might be in contexts where they currently assume infallibility.

**Option 2: Validate in the COPY stage**
- Options could be:
  - (a) When decoding text to Value::Range, validate lower <= upper and return Err from decode_text. That would make COPY fail with a proper error at decode time.
  - (b) In pgcopy, after decode_text but before into_datum, check if it's a Range and validate it, then call into_datum.
- Pros: Localized change. into_datum stays as is. Only the COPY path gets the fix.
- Cons: Duplicates validation logic (we'd need to validate in the pgrepr layer or in pgcopy, and the repr layer already has it in push_range). Or we could validate by trying to convert and catching the error—but that's awkward. Actually the cleanest "validate in COPY stage" would be: when we have Value::Range, before calling into_datum, convert to repr Range and call something that validates. That might mean we need a validated conversion path. Or we add validation in strconv::parse_range / Value::decode_text for ranges so that misordered bounds produce an Err from decode_text. Then COPY already handles Err from decode_text (it returns "unable to decode column: ..."). So we wouldn't need to change into_datum at all—we'd just add validation when parsing the range text into a Value. That way the Value never represents an invalid range.

Let me think about where that validation would go. The parser produces a pgrepr Range. Does pgrepr have a notion of "valid" range (lower <= upper)? We could add a check in Value::decode_text for Type::Range: after parse_range, walk the bounds and compare. But the pgrepr Range type might store bounds in a way that's generic (element type is Box<Value>). So we'd need to compare two Value elements. That might be doable for the known element types (int4, int8, etc.). Or we could do the validation inside into_datum by returning Result—then the only place that needs to know how to validate is the code that converts to Datum (which already has push_range). So actually Option 1 (into_datum returns Result) avoids duplicating validation logic.

Let me check call sites of into_datum to assess the scope of Option 1.
</details></think>

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

So we have:

**Call sites of `Value::into_datum` (pgrepr):**
1. `pgwire/src/protocol.rs:1330` - param.into_datum (bind parameters)
2. `pgcopy/src/copy.rs:560` - value.into_datum (COPY - the one that panics)
3. `environmentd/src/http/sql.rs:1397` - param.into_datum (HTTP SQL params)
4. `storage-operators/src/oneshot_source/csv.rs:229` - value.into_datum (CSV source)
5. `pgrepr/src/value.rs` - internal recursive calls for Array, List, Map, Range elements

The other `into_datum` hits are different - they're on other types (e.g. `repr::adt::jsonb` has its own `into_datum`, scalar.rs, expr - those are different APIs).

So the **external** callers of `Value::into_datum` are:
- pgwire protocol (bind)
- pgcopy (COPY)
- environmentd http sql (params)
- storage-operators csv

If we make `Value::into_datum` return `Result<Datum<'a>, E>`, we need an error type. `InvalidRangeError` is in repr. So we could have the Result use something that can represent range errors (and any future validation errors from other variants). Callers would need to handle the Result.

**Option 2: Validate in COPY stage**

We could validate when we have a Range value. Options:
- **2a) In `Value::decode_text` for Type::Range**: After `parse_range`, before returning the Value, validate that the bounds are ordered. To do that we'd need to compare the two bounds. The parsed range has elements as `Box<Value>`. We could add a method on Value for partial ordering or a "validate range bounds" that only works for range types. But comparing two arbitrary Values for "less than or equal" might be nontrivial (we need the same logic as the repr). Actually the repr already has the invariant—we could convert to repr Range and check. So: decode_text for Range could build the same structure that into_datum would build, and call push_range in a temporary packer, and if it returns Err, map that to a decode error. That would duplicate the conversion logic (we're doing into_datum's job inside decode_text just to validate). Messy.
- **2b) Validate in parse_range / strconv**: The strconv parser produces a range with bounds. We could add a validation step there that takes the element type and checks lower <= upper. That would require strconv to know about ordering for each element type (int4, int8, date, etc.). Doable but pushes validation into the parser layer.
- **2c) Validate only in the COPY path**: In pgcopy, after `Value::decode_text` returns Ok(value), if the value is a Range, call a new method like `value.validate_range()` or try to convert and catch. If we had `into_datum` return Result we're back to option 1. If we add a separate "validate range" that converts Value::Range to repr and checks, we're duplicating the conversion. The clean way to "validate in COPY" without changing into_datum is to make **decode_text** for ranges fail when bounds are misordered. So we need to validate in the parser or in Value::decode_text. For decode_text we have Value::Range(r). We need to check r's bounds. The pgrepr Range type - let me see what it looks like.

Actually re-reading the user's suggestion: "Alternatively we somehow check in the copy stage". So they're open to either (1) into_datum returns Result and handle at every call, or (2) check in the copy stage.

For (2), the minimal change is: in the COPY text decoder, when we have decoded a Value and the type is Range, before calling into_datum we could try to validate. The only way to validate with existing code is to actually run the conversion (push_range) and see if it errors. So we'd need either:
- into_datum to return Result (so we can propagate the error in COPY), or
- a separate function like `try_into_datum` that returns Result, and use that in COPY (and keep into_datum for other callers, but then into_datum would still unwrap for Range—so we'd have two code paths for Range in into_datum, which is bad).

So if we want to "check in the copy stage" without changing into_datum's signature, we'd need to validate *before* into_datum. That means validating the Value::Range representation. So we need a function that takes Value::Range (and element type) and returns Result<(), InvalidRangeError>. That function would need to convert bounds to something comparable. The only place that has the comparison logic is push_range (which compares Datums). So we'd do: build the repr Range from Value::Range (same as into_datum does), then call something that only validates (e.g. a new Range::validate_bounds or use push_range on a temporary packer and discard the result). So we're still doing the same work as into_datum; we're just checking first and then calling into_datum. That's redundant. Unless we add validation in the parser (strconv::parse_range) by comparing the two bound Values. Then we never produce an invalid Value::Range. So:

**Option 2 (validate earlier) - concrete approach:** In `strconv::parse_range` or immediately after in `Value::decode_text` for Type::Range, after parsing, check that lower bound <= upper bound (when both present). If not, return Err. Then into_datum never sees an invalid range. We need to implement comparison for the element types (int4, int8, date, numeric, timestamp, timestamptz). The Value type holds Box<Value> for the bounds. So we need a way to compare two Values of a given type. That might already exist (for ordering). Let me not digress—the point is we could add validation at decode time so that invalid ranges never become Value::Range, and then into_datum doesn't need to change. The downside is duplicating the "what is valid" logic (currently only in push_range) into the parser/decode layer, and we need to implement bound comparison for all range element types in pgrepr.

I'll summarize both options for the user with pros/cons.
</details></think>

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

I also notice that Value::into_datum has another .unwrap() for arrays: try_push_array can fail. So making into_datum return Result might be the right design anyway—there can be more than one fallible conversion. Let me give a clear comparison to the user.
</details></think>

---


Here’s a concise way to think about the two approaches.

---

## 1. Make `into_datum` return a `Result`

**Idea:** Change `Value::into_datum` to something like `Value::into_datum(...) -> Result<Datum<'a>, InvalidRangeError>` (or a small error enum), use `?` instead of `.unwrap()` for `push_range`, and have every caller handle the `Result`.

**Call sites that need updates (only the ones that call `pgrepr::Value::into_datum`):**

- **pgcopy** – COPY text/CSV: already in a `Result` chain; you’d `?` and the error becomes “unable to decode column: …” (you can map the error to a clear message).
- **pgwire** – bind parameters: would need to map the error to a protocol error and send it to the client.
- **environmentd http/sql** – SQL params: same as pgwire.
- **storage-operators csv** – CSV source: would need to decide how to surface the error (e.g. fail the source with a clear error).
- **pgrepr/value.rs** – recursive calls for `Array` / `List` / `Map` / `Range` elements: those call `into_datum` on inner values; if the signature returns `Result`, you’d propagate with `?` (and the outer `into_datum` would also return `Result`).

**Pros:**

- One place does the real validation: `push_range` in repr. No duplicate “what is a valid range?” logic.
- Any other conversion that can fail (e.g. you already have `try_push_array` with `.unwrap()` in `into_datum`) can use the same `Result` and stop panicking.
- Call sites that already work with `Result` (like COPY) get a proper error path with no panic.

**Cons:**

- Every caller of `into_datum` must handle the `Result`. There are only a handful of call sites, but it’s a signature change.
- You need a concrete error type (e.g. from `repr` or a small enum in `pgrepr`) that COPY/pgwire/http/csv can map to user-facing errors.

---

## 2. Validate in the COPY stage (or at decode time)

**Idea:** Never allow an invalid range to become a `Value::Range`, so `into_datum` never sees misordered bounds.

**Ways to do it:**

- **A) In COPY only:** After `decode_text` returns `Ok(value)` and before `into_datum`, check “if this is a range, validate bounds.” To reuse existing logic you’d have to either (i) try the conversion and handle failure (which effectively means having a fallible conversion somewhere, like a `try_into_datum`), or (ii) reimplement “lower ≤ upper” for range bounds in the COPY layer. (i) leads you back toward Option 1; (ii) duplicates logic that already lives in `push_range`.
- **B) At decode time:** In `Value::decode_text` for `Type::Range`, after `strconv::parse_range`, validate lower ≤ upper and return `Err(...)` if not. COPY already turns `decode_text` errors into “unable to decode column: …”, so no panic and a clear error. To implement the check you must compare the two bound `Value`s (e.g. for int4range, the two `Value::Int4`). So you’d add a small “compare two bounds for this element type” helper and call it from decode. That keeps invalid ranges out of `Value` and leaves `into_datum` as-is.

**Pros:**

- No change to `into_datum` or its call sites.
- Fix is localized to the path that currently panics (decode/COPY).

**Cons:**

- You duplicate the “valid range” rule: it stays in `push_range` for INSERT and is reimplemented (or re-checked) at decode time for COPY. If someone adds a new range element type or changes the rule, both places must stay in sync.
- Option B requires bound comparison for all range element types in pgrepr (int4, int8, date, numeric, timestamp, timestamptz). Doable but more code in the decode layer.

---

## Recommendation

- **If you want a minimal, localized fix:** Option 2B (validate in `Value::decode_text` for `Type::Range` and return `Err` when bounds are misordered) fixes the panic and gives COPY a proper error without touching `into_datum` or other callers. You pay with a bit of duplicated validation and bound-comparison code in pgrepr.
- **If you prefer a single source of truth and are okay updating a few call sites:** Option 1 (make `into_datum` return `Result` and handle it everywhere) is cleaner long term: validation stays in repr, and any future fallible conversions in `into_datum` (e.g. arrays) can use the same mechanism instead of panicking. The number of call sites is small, so the change is manageable.

So: “validate in the copy stage” is best implemented by validating at **decode** time (Option 2B) so invalid ranges never reach `into_datum`. The alternative is to make the conversion itself fallible (Option 1) and handle the result at every call, which is the more robust design if you expect more fallible conversions in `into_datum` over time.

---

_**User**_

Ok, I think we go with option 1. Let's make a plan to do it

---

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

So `into_bounds` is `Fn(D) -> O` - it takes one argument and returns O. So if we change `into_datum` to return `Result<Datum, E>`, then `elem.into_datum(buf, elem_pg_type)` returns `Result<Datum, E>`. So we can't pass that closure directly to `into_bounds` because the closure would need to return `Result<Datum, E>` but `into_bounds` expects `O` (e.g. `Datum`). So we have two options:
1. Build the range manually: get lower and upper from the pgrepr Range, call into_datum on each, use ? to propagate, then construct the repr Range and push_range.
2. Add a `try_into_bounds` or similar that accepts a closure returning Result.

Option 1: We need to get the bounds from the pgrepr Range. Let me check the structure - the pgrepr Range is probably from postgres-types. So we'd do something like: convert each bound with into_datum (which returns Result), collect with ?, then build Range<Datum> and push_range(range)?.

I'll include in the plan that for the Range (and Array) branches we need to handle the fallible conversion—either by manually building the range from bounds and using ? on each into_datum, or by introducing a try_into_bounds if the pgrepr range type allows. I'll leave the implementation detail and just say "propagate errors from recursive into_datum and from push_range/try_push_array".
</details></think>

---


Summary of the plan and where it lives:

---

**Plan doc:** `doc/developer/into-datum-result-plan.md`

**High-level steps:**

1. **Error type**  
   Add something like `IntoDatumError` in pgrepr that wraps `InvalidRangeError` and `InvalidArrayError` (from mz-repr), with `Display`, `Error`, and `From` impls.

2. **`Value::into_datum`**  
   Change signature to  
   `into_datum(...) -> Result<Datum<'a>, IntoDatumError>`.  
   - Replace `.unwrap()` on `try_push_array` and `push_range` with `?`.  
   - For **Array/List/Map**, make recursive `into_datum` calls propagate (e.g. collect into `Result<Vec<_>, _>>` or loop with `?`).  
   - For **Range**, `into_bounds` currently takes a closure that returns `Datum`; once `into_datum` returns `Result`, build the repr `Range` in a fallible way (e.g. manually convert each bound with `?`, then `push_range(range)?`).

3. **`decode_text_into_row`**  
   In the `Type::Range` branch, build the range using the same fallible conversion and use `?` on `push_range` instead of `.unwrap()`.

4. **Call sites**  
   - **pgcopy** (text + CSV): match on `into_datum`’s `Result` and turn `Err` into an `io::Error` with a clear message.  
   - **pgwire**: bind params — on `Err` from `into_datum`, return a protocol error (e.g. `INVALID_PARAMETER_VALUE`).  
   - **environmentd http/sql**: same idea, return the appropriate HTTP/SQL error.  
   - **storage-operators csv**: use `into_datum(...)?` (or equivalent) and map the error into the existing error type used there.

5. **Tests**  
   Add a COPY test that sends an invalid range (e.g. `[7, 3)`) and asserts a proper error message and no panic.

The doc has code-level notes, the exact branches to touch, and the recommended implementation order. If you want, we can walk through implementing one of the steps (e.g. the error type and the Range branch) next.

---

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

So sqllogictest has a different format - it uses "statement ok", "query error", etc. The privileges test uses "COPY atest2 FROM stdin" and expects "db error". So in sqllogictest, COPY FROM stdin might be supported with a special syntax. Let me check how sqllogictest runs COPY - does it support inline data?
</details></think>

        "contents": "                }\n            }\n            Record::Query {\n                sql,\n                output,\n                location,\n            } =&gt; {\n                self.run_query(sql, output, location.clone(), in_transaction)\n                    .await\n            }\n            Record::Simple {\n                conn,\n                user,\n                password,\n                sql,\n                sort,\n                output,\n                location,\n                ..\n            } =&gt; {\n                self.run_simple(\n                    *conn,\n                    *user,\n                    *password,\n                    sql,\n                    sort.clone(),\n                    output,\n                    location.clone(),\n                )\n                .await\n            }\n            Record::Copy {\n                table_name,\n                tsv_path,\n            } =&gt; {\n                let tsv = tokio::fs::read(tsv_path).await?;\n                let copy = self\n                    .client\n                    .copy_in(&amp;*format!(\"COPY {} FROM STDIN\", table_name))\n                    .await?;\n                tokio::pin!(copy);\n                copy.send(bytes::Bytes::from(tsv)).await?;\n                copy.finish().await?;\n                Ok(Outcome::Success)\n            }\n            _ =&gt; Ok(Outcome::Success),\n        }\n    }\n\n    async fn run_statement&lt;'r&gt;(\n        &amp;self,\n        expected_error: Option&lt;&amp;'r str&gt;",

        "contents": "            // this is some cockroach-specific thing, we don't care\n            \"subtest\" | \"user\" | \"kv-batch-size\" =&gt; self.parse_record(),\n\n            \"mode\" =&gt; {\n                self.mode = match words.next() {\n                    Some(\"cockroach\") =&gt; Mode::Cockroach,\n                    Some(\"standard\") | Some(\"sqlite\") =&gt; Mode::Standard,\n                    other =&gt; bail!(\"unknown parse mode: {:?}\", other),\n                };\n                self.parse_record()\n            }\n\n            \"copy\" =&gt; Ok(Record::Copy {\n                table_name: words\n                    .next()\n                    .ok_or_else(|| anyhow!(\"load directive missing table name\"))?,\n                tsv_path: words\n                    .next()\n                    .ok_or_else(|| anyhow!(\"load directive missing TSV path\"))?,\n            }),\n\n            \"reset-server\" =&gt; Ok(Record::ResetServer),\n\n            other =&gt; bail!(\n                \"Unexpected start of record on line {}: {}\",\n                line_number,\n                other\n            ),\n        }\n    }\n\n    pub fn parse_records(&amp;mut self) -&gt; Result&lt;Vec&lt;Record&lt;'a&gt;&gt;, anyhow::Error&gt; {\n        let mut records = vec![];\n        loop {\n            match self.parse_record()? {\n                Record::Halt =&gt; break,\n                record =&gt; records.push(record),\n            }\n        }\n        Ok(records)\n    }\n\n    fn parse_statement(\n        &amp;mut self,",

        "contents": "#[derive(Debug, Clone, PartialEq, Eq)]\npub enum Record&lt;'a&gt; {\n    // A &#96;statement&#96; directive.\n    Statement {\n        location: Location,\n        expected_error: Option&lt;&amp;'a str&gt;,\n        rows_affected: Option&lt;u64&gt;,\n        sql: &amp;'a str,\n    },\n    /// A &#96;query&#96; directive.\n    Query {\n        location: Location,\n        sql: &amp;'a str,\n        output: Result&lt;QueryOutput&lt;'a&gt;, &amp;'a str&gt;,\n    },\n    /// A &#96;simple&#96; directive.\n    Simple {\n        location: Location,\n        conn: Option&lt;&amp;'a str&gt;,\n        user: Option&lt;&amp;'a str&gt;,\n        password: Option&lt;&amp;'a str&gt;,\n        sql: &amp;'a str,\n        sort: Sort,\n        output: Output,\n        output_str: &amp;'a str,\n    },\n    /// A &#96;hash-threshold&#96; directive.\n    HashThreshold { threshold: u64 },\n    /// A &#96;halt&#96; directive.\n    Halt,\n    /// A &#96;copy&#96; directive.\n    Copy {\n        table_name: &amp;'a str,\n        tsv_path: &amp;'a str,\n    },\n    /// A &#96;reset-server&#96; directive\n    ResetServer,\n}\n\n/// Specifies the dialect of a sqllogictest file. Different sqllogictest runners\n/// have slightly different behavior.\n#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq)]\npub enum Mode {\n    /// In &#96;Standard&#96; mode, expected query output is formatted so that every",

      "codeBlock": {
        "relativeWorkspacePath": "./src/sqllogictest/src/runner.rs",

            "line": 2060,
            "column": 33
          }
        },
        "contents": "const PRINT_INDENT: usize = 4;\n\nfn print_record(config: &amp;RunConfig&lt;'_&gt;, record: &amp;Record) {\n    match record {\n        Record::Statement { sql, .. } | Record::Query { sql, .. } =&gt; {\n            print_sql(config.stdout, sql, None)\n        }\n        Record::Simple { conn, sql, .. } =&gt; print_sql(config.stdout, sql, *conn),\n        Record::Copy {\n            table_name,\n            tsv_path,\n        } =&gt; {\n            writeln!(\n                config.stdout,\n                \"{}slt copy {} from {}\",\n                \" \".repeat(PRINT_INDENT),\n                table_name,\n                tsv_path\n            )\n        }\n        Record::ResetServer =&gt; {\n            writeln!(config.stdout, \"{}reset-server\", \" \".repeat(PRINT_INDENT))\n        }\n        Record::Halt =&gt; {\n            writeln!(config.stdout, \"{}halt\", \" \".repeat(PRINT_INDENT))\n        }\n        Record::HashThreshold { threshold } =&gt; {\n            writeln!(\n                config.stdout,\n                \"{}hash-threshold {}\",\n                \" \".repeat(PRINT_INDENT),\n                threshold\n            )\n        }\n    }\n}\n\nfn print_sql_if&lt;'a&gt;(stdout: &amp;'a dyn WriteFmt, sql: &amp;str, cond: bool) {\n    if cond {\n        print_sql(stdout, sql, None)\n    }\n}\n\nfn print_sql&lt;'a&gt;(stdout: &amp;'a dyn WriteFmt, sql: &amp;str, conn: Option&lt;&amp;str&gt;) {\n    let text = if let Some(conn) = conn {\n        format!(\"[conn={}] {}\", conn, sql)\n    } else {\n        sql.to_string()\n    };\n    writeln!(stdout, \"{}\", util::indent(&amp;text, PRINT_INDENT))\n}\n\n/// Regular expressions for matching error messages that should force a plan failure\n/// in an inconsistent view outcome into a warning if the corresponding query succeeds.\nconst INCONSISTENT_VIEW_OUTCOME_WARNING_REGEXPS: [&amp;str; 9] = [\n    // The following are unfixable errors in indexed views given our\n    // current constraints.\n    \"cannot materialize call to\",",
