    {
      "codeBlock": {
        "relativeWorkspacePath": "./src/sqllogictest/src/runner.rs",
        "range": {
          "startPosition": {},
          "endPosition": {
            "line": 47,
            "column": 49
          }
        },
        "contents": "// Copyright Materialize, Inc. and contributors. All rights reserved.\n//\n// Use of this software is governed by the Business Source License\n// included in the LICENSE file.\n//\n// As of the Change Date specified in that file, in accordance with\n// the Business Source License, use of this software will be governed\n// by the Apache License, Version 2.0.\n\n//! The Materialize-specific runner for sqllogictest.\n//!\n//! slt tests expect a serialized execution of sql statements and queries.\n//! To get the same results in materialize we track current_timestamp and increment it whenever we execute a statement.\n//!\n//! The high-level workflow is:\n//!   for each record in the test file:\n//!     if record is a sql statement:\n//!       run sql in postgres, observe changes and copy them to materialize using LocalInput::Updates(..)\n//!       advance current_timestamp\n//!       promise to never send updates for times &lt; current_timestamp using LocalInput::Watermark(..)\n//!       compare to expected results\n//!       if wrong, bail out and stop processing this file\n//!     if record is a sql query:\n//!       peek query at current_timestamp\n//!       compare to expected results\n//!       if wrong, record the error\n\nuse std::collections::BTreeMap;\nuse std::error::Error;\nuse std::fs::{File, OpenOptions};\nuse std::io::{Read, Seek, SeekFrom, Write};\nuse std::net::{IpAddr, Ipv4Addr, SocketAddr};\nuse std::path::Path;\nuse std::sync::Arc;\nuse std::sync::LazyLock;\nuse std::time::Duration;\nuse std::{env, fmt, ops, str, thread};\n\nuse anyhow::{anyhow, bail};\nuse bytes::BytesMut;\nuse chrono::{DateTime, NaiveDateTime, NaiveTime, Utc};\nuse fallible_iterator::FallibleIterator;\nuse futures::sink::SinkExt;\nuse itertools::Itertools;\nuse maplit::btreemap;\nuse md5::{Digest, Md5};\nuse mz_adapter_types::bootstrap_builtin_cluster_config::{\n    ANALYTICS_CLUSTER_DEFAULT_REPLICATION_FACTOR,",
        "signatures": {},
        "detailedLines": [
          {
            "text": "// Copyright Materialize, Inc. and contributors. All rights reserved.",
            "lineNumber": 1
          },
          {
            "text": "//",
            "lineNumber": 2
          },
          {
            "text": "// Use of this software is governed by the Business Source License",
            "lineNumber": 3
          },
          {
            "text": "// included in the LICENSE file.",
            "lineNumber": 4
          },
          {
            "text": "//",
            "lineNumber": 5
          },
          {
            "text": "// As of the Change Date specified in that file, in accordance with",
            "lineNumber": 6
          },
          {
            "text": "// the Business Source License, use of this software will be governed",
            "lineNumber": 7
          },
          {
            "text": "// by the Apache License, Version 2.0.",
            "lineNumber": 8
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 9
          },
          {
            "text": "//! The Materialize-specific runner for sqllogictest.",
            "lineNumber": 10
          },
          {
            "text": "//!",
            "lineNumber": 11
          },
          {
            "text": "//! slt tests expect a serialized execution of sql statements and queries.",
            "lineNumber": 12
          },
          {
            "text": "//! To get the same results in materialize we track current_timestamp and increment it whenever we execute a statement.",
            "lineNumber": 13
          },
          {
            "text": "//!",
            "lineNumber": 14
          },
          {
            "text": "//! The high-level workflow is:",
            "lineNumber": 15
          },
          {
            "text": "//!   for each record in the test file:",
            "lineNumber": 16
          },
          {
            "text": "//!     if record is a sql statement:",
            "lineNumber": 17
          },
          {
            "text": "//!       run sql in postgres, observe changes and copy them to materialize using LocalInput::Updates(..)",
            "lineNumber": 18
          },
          {
            "text": "//!       advance current_timestamp",
            "lineNumber": 19
          },
          {
            "text": "//!       promise to never send updates for times &lt; current_timestamp using LocalInput::Watermark(..)",
            "lineNumber": 20
          },
          {
            "text": "//!       compare to expected results",
            "lineNumber": 21
          },
          {
            "text": "//!       if wrong, bail out and stop processing this file",
            "lineNumber": 22
          },
          {
            "text": "//!     if record is a sql query:",
            "lineNumber": 23
          },
          {
            "text": "//!       peek query at current_timestamp",
            "lineNumber": 24
          },
          {
            "text": "//!       compare to expected results",
            "lineNumber": 25
          },
          {
            "text": "//!       if wrong, record the error",
            "lineNumber": 26
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 27

      "codeBlock": {

        "range": {

            "line": 131,
            "column": 67
          }
        },
        "contents": "        }\n    }\n\n    pub fn parse_record(&amp;mut self) -&gt; Result&lt;Record&lt;'a&gt;, anyhow::Error&gt; {\n        if self.is_done() {\n            return Ok(Record::Halt);\n        }\n\n        let line_number = self.curline;\n\n        static COMMENT_AND_LINE_REGEX: LazyLock&lt;Regex&gt; =\n            LazyLock::new(|| Regex::new(\"(#[^\\n]*)?\\r?(\\n|$)\").unwrap());\n        let first_line = self.split_at(&amp;COMMENT_AND_LINE_REGEX)?.trim();\n\n        if first_line.is_empty() {\n            // query starts on the next line\n            return self.parse_record();\n        }\n\n        let mut words = first_line.split(' ').peekable();\n        match words.next().unwrap() {\n            \"statement\" =&gt; self.parse_statement(words, first_line),\n\n            \"query\" =&gt; self.parse_query(words, first_line),\n\n            \"simple\" =&gt; self.parse_simple(words),\n\n            \"hash-threshold\" =&gt; {\n                let threshold = words\n                    .next()\n                    .ok_or_else(|| anyhow!(\"missing threshold in: {}\", first_line))?\n                    .parse::&lt;u64&gt;()\n                    .map_err(|err| anyhow!(\"invalid threshold ({}) in: {}\", err, first_line))?;\n                Ok(Record::HashThreshold { threshold })\n            }\n\n            // we'll follow the postgresql version of all these tests\n            \"skipif\" =&gt; {\n                match words.next().unwrap() {\n                    \"postgresql\" =&gt; {\n                        // discard next record\n                        self.parse_record()?;\n                        self.parse_record()\n                    }\n                    _ =&gt; self.parse_record(),\n                }\n            }\n            \"onlyif\" =&gt; {\n                match words.next().unwrap() {\n                    \"postgresql\" =&gt; self.parse_record(),\n                    _ =&gt; {\n                        // discard next record\n                        self.parse_record()?;\n                        self.parse_record()\n                    }\n                }\n            }\n\n            \"halt\" =&gt; Ok(Record::Halt),\n\n            // this is some cockroach-specific thing, we don't care",
        "signatures": {},

        "contents": "pub async fn rewrite_file(runner: &amp;mut Runner&lt;'_&gt;, filename: &amp;Path) -&gt; Result&lt;(), anyhow::Error&gt; {\n    runner.reset_database().await?;\n\n    let mut file = OpenOptions::new().read(true).write(true).open(filename)?;\n\n    let mut input = String::new();\n    file.read_to_string(&amp;mut input)?;\n\n    let mut buf = RewriteBuffer::new(&amp;input);\n\n    let mut parser = crate::parser::Parser::new(filename.to_str().unwrap_or(\"\"), &amp;input);\n    writeln!(runner.config.stdout, \"--- {}\", filename.display());\n    let mut in_transaction = false;\n\n    fn append_values_output(\n        buf: &amp;mut RewriteBuffer,\n        input: &amp;String,\n        expected_output: &amp;str,\n        mode: &amp;Mode,\n        types: &amp;Vec&lt;Type&gt;,\n        column_names: Option&lt;&amp;Vec&lt;ColumnName&gt;&gt;,\n        actual_output: &amp;Vec&lt;String&gt;,\n        multiline: bool,\n    ) {\n        buf.append_header(input, expected_output, column_names);\n\n        for (i, row) in",

      "codeBlock": {

        "contents": "pub async fn run_string(\n    runner: &amp;mut Runner&lt;'_&gt;,\n    source: &amp;str,\n    input: &amp;str,\n) -&gt; Result&lt;Outcomes, anyhow::Error&gt; {\n    runner.reset_database().await?;\n\n    let mut outcomes = Outcomes::default();\n    let mut parser = crate::parser::Parser::new(source, input);\n    // Transactions are currently relatively slow. Since sqllogictest runs in a single connection\n    // there should be no difference in having longer running transactions.\n    let mut in_transaction = false;\n    writeln!(runner.config.stdout, \"--- {}\", source);\n\n    for record in parser.parse_records()? {\n        // In maximal-verbosity mode, print the query before attempting to run\n        // it. Running the query might panic, so it is important to print out\n        // what query we are trying to run *before* we panic.\n        if runner.config.verbosity &gt;= 2 {\n            print_record(runner.config, &amp;record);\n        }\n\n        let outcome = runner\n            .run_record(&amp;record, &amp;mut in_transaction)\n            .await\n            .map_err(|err| format!(\"In {}:\\n{}\", source, err))\n            .unwrap();\n\n        // Print warnings and failures in verbose mode.\n        if runner.config.verbosity &gt;= 1 &amp;&amp; !outcome.success() {\n            if runner.config.verbosity &lt; 2",

    {
      "codeBlock": {
        "relativeWorkspacePath": "./src/sqllogictest/src/parser.rs",
        "range": {

          }

        "contents": ",\n        })\n    }\n\n    fn parse_simple(\n        &amp;mut self,\n        mut words: std::iter::Peekable&lt;impl Iterator&lt;Item = &amp;'a str&gt;&gt;,\n    ) -&gt; Result&lt;Record&lt;'a&gt;, anyhow::Error&gt; {\n        let location = self.location();\n        let mut conn = None;\n        let mut user = None;\n        let mut password = None;\n        let mut multiline = false;\n        let mut sort = Sort::No;\n        if let Some(options) = words.next() {\n            for option in options.split(',') {\n                if let Some(value) = option.strip_prefix(\"conn=\") {\n                    conn = Some(value);\n                } else if let Some(value) = option.strip_prefix(\"user=\") {\n                    user = Some(value);\n                } else if let Some(value) = option.strip_prefix(\"password=\") {\n                    password = Some(value);\n                } else if option == \"rowsort\" {\n                    sort = Sort::Row;\n                } else if option == \"multiline\" {\n                    multiline = true;\n                } else {\n                    bail!(\"Unrecognized option {:?} in {:?}\", option, options);\n                }\n            }\n        }\n        if user.is_some() &amp;&amp; conn.is_none() {\n            bail!(\"cannot set user without also setting conn\");\n        }\n        if password.is_some() &amp;&amp; user.is_none() {\n            bail!(\"cannot set password without also setting user\");\n        }\n        let sql = self.split_at(&amp;QUERY_OUTPUT_REGEX)?;\n        let output_str = self\n            .split_at(if multiline {\n                &amp;EOF_REGEX\n            } else {\n                &amp;DOUBLE_LINE_REGEX\n            })?\n            .trim_start();\n        let output = if multiline {\n            Output::Values({\n                let mut v = vec![output_str.to_owned()];",

        "contents": "    fn parse_statement(\n        &amp;mut self,\n        mut words: impl Iterator&lt;Item = &amp;'a str&gt;,\n        first_line: &amp;'a str,\n    ) -&gt; Result&lt;Record&lt;'a&gt;, anyhow::Error&gt; {\n        let location = self.location();\n        let mut expected_error = None;\n        let mut rows_affected = None;\n        match words.next() {\n            Some(\"count\") =&gt; {\n                rows_affected = Some(\n                    words\n                        .next()\n                        .ok_or_else(|| anyhow!(\"missing count of rows affected\"))?\n                        .parse::&lt;u64&gt;()\n                        .map_err(|err| anyhow!(\"parsing count of rows affected: {}\", err))?,\n                );\n            }\n            Some(\"ok\") | Some(\"OK\") =&gt; (),\n            Some(\"error\") =&gt; expected_error = Some(parse_expected_error(first_line)),\n            _ =&gt; bail!(\"invalid statement disposition: {}\", first_line),\n        };\n        let sql = self.split_at(&amp;DOUBLE_LINE_REGEX)?;\n        Ok(Record::Statement {\n            expected_error,\n            rows_affected,\n            sql,\n            location,\n        })\n    }\n\n    fn parse_query(\n        &amp;mut self,\n        mut words:",

        "contents": "    fn parse_query(\n        &amp;mut self,\n        mut words: std::iter::Peekable&lt;impl Iterator&lt;Item = &amp;'a str&gt;&gt;,\n        first_line: &amp;'a str,\n    ) -&gt; Result&lt;Record&lt;'a&gt;, anyhow::Error&gt; {\n        let location = self.location();\n        if words.peek() == Some(&amp;\"error\") {\n            let error = parse_expected_error(first_line);\n            let sql = self.split_at(&amp;DOUBLE_LINE_REGEX)?;\n            return Ok(Record::Query {\n                sql,\n                output: Err(error),\n                location,\n            });\n        }\n\n        let types = words.next().map_or(Ok(vec![]), parse_types)?;\n        let mut sort = Sort::No;\n        let mut check_column_names = false;\n        let mut multiline = false;\n        if let Some(options) = words.next() {\n            for option in options.split(',') {\n                match option {\n                    \"nosort\" =&gt; sort = Sort::No,\n                    \"rowsort\" =&gt; sort = Sort::Row,\n                    \"valuesort\" =&gt; sort = Sort::Value,\n                    \"colnames\" =&gt; check_column_names = true,\n                    \"multiline\" =&gt; multiline = true,\n                    other =&gt; {\n                        if other.starts_with(\"partialsort\") {\n                            // TODO(jamii) https://github.com/cockroachdb/cockroach/blob/d2f7fbf5dd1fc1a099bbad790a2e1f7c60a66cc3/pkg/sql/logictest/logic.go#L153\n                            // partialsort has comma-separated arguments so our parsing is totally broken\n                            // luckily it always comes last in the existing tests, so we can just bail out for now\n                            sort = Sort::Row;\n                            break;\n                        } else {\n                            bail!(\"Unrecognized option {:?} in {:?}\", other, options);\n                        }\n                    }\n                };\n            }\n        }\n        if multiline &amp;&amp; (check_column_names || sort.yes()) {\n            bail!(\"multiline option is incompatible with all other options\");",

    {
      "codeBlock": {
        "relativeWorkspacePath": "./src/sqllogictest/src/runner.rs",
        "range": {
          "startPosition": {

          "endPosition": {
            "line": 2005,
            "column": 15
          }
        },
        "contents": "async fn connect(\n    addr: SocketAddr,\n    user: Option&lt;&amp;str&gt;,\n    password: Option&lt;&amp;str&gt;,\n) -&gt; Result&lt;tokio_postgres::Client, tokio_postgres::Error&gt; {\n    let mut config = tokio_postgres::Config::new();\n    config.host(addr.ip().to_string());\n    config.port(addr.port());\n    config.user(user.unwrap_or(\"materialize\"));\n    if let Some(password) = password {\n        config.password(password);\n    }\n    let (client, connection) = config.connect(NoTls).await?;\n\n    task::spawn(|| \"sqllogictest_connect\", async move {\n        if let Err(e) = connection.await {\n            eprintln!(\"connection error: {}\", e);\n        }\n    });\n    Ok(client)\n}\n\npub trait WriteFmt {\n    fn write_fmt(&amp;self, fmt: fmt::Arguments&lt;'_&gt;);\n}\n\npub struct RunConfig&lt;'a&gt; {\n    pub stdout: &amp;'a dyn WriteFmt,\n    pub stderr: &amp;'a dyn WriteFmt,\n    pub verbosity: u8,\n    pub postgres_url: String,\n    pub prefix: String,\n    pub no_fail: bool,\n    pub fail_fast: bool,\n    pub auto_index_tables: bool,\n    pub auto_index_selects: bool,\n    pub auto_transactions: bool,\n    pub enable_table_keys: bool,\n    pub orchestrator_process_wrapper: Option&lt;String&gt;,\n    pub tracing: TracingCliArgs,\n    pub tracing_handle: TracingHandle,\n    pub system_parameter_defaults: BTreeMap&lt;String, String&gt;,\n    /// Persist state is handled specially because:\n    /// - Persist background workers do not necessarily shut down immediately once the server is\n    ///   shut down, and may panic if their storage is deleted out from under them.\n    /// - It's safe for different databases to reference the same state: all data is scoped by UUID.\n    pub persist_dir: TempDir,\n    pub replicas: usize,\n    pub replica_size: String,\n}\n\n/// Indentation used for verbose output of SQL statements.\nconst PRINT_INDENT: usize = 4;\n\nfn print_record",
        "signatures": {},
        "detailedLines": [

          {
            "text": "    pub tracing: TracingCliArgs,",
            "lineNumber": 1991
          },
          {
            "text": "    pub tracing_handle: TracingHandle,",
            "lineNumber": 1992
          },
          {
            "text": "    pub system_parameter_defaults: BTreeMap&lt;String, String&gt;,",
            "lineNumber": 1993
          },
          {
            "text": "    /// Persist state is handled specially because:",
            "lineNumber": 1994
          },
          {
            "text": "    /// - Persist background workers do not necessarily shut down immediately once the server is",
            "lineNumber": 1995
          },
          {
            "text": "    ///   shut down, and may panic if their storage is deleted out from under them.",
            "lineNumber": 1996
          },
          {
            "text": "    /// - It's safe for different databases to reference the same state: all data is scoped by UUID.",
            "lineNumber": 1997
          },
          {
            "text": "    pub persist_dir: TempDir,",
            "lineNumber": 1998
          },
          {
            "text": "    pub replicas: usize,",
            "lineNumber": 1999
          },
          {
            "text": "    pub replica_size: String,",
            "lineNumber": 2000
          },
          {
            "text": "}",
            "lineNumber": 2001
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 2002
          },
          {
            "text": "/// Indentation used for verbose output of SQL statements.",
            "lineNumber": 2003
          },
          {
            "text": "const PRINT_INDENT: usize = 4;",

        "contents": "    async fn run_statement&lt;'r&gt;(\n        &amp;self,\n        expected_error: Option&lt;&amp;'r str&gt;,\n        expected_rows_affected: Option&lt;u64&gt;,\n        sql: &amp;'r str,\n        location: Location,\n    ) -&gt; Result&lt;Outcome&lt;'r&gt;, anyhow::Error&gt; {\n        static UNSUPPORTED_INDEX_STATEMENT_REGEX: LazyLock&lt;Regex&gt; =\n            LazyLock::new(|| Regex::new(\"^(CREATE UNIQUE INDEX|REINDEX)\").unwrap());\n        if UNSUPPORTED_INDEX_STATEMENT_REGEX.is_match(sql) {\n            // sure, we totally made you an index\n            return Ok(Outcome::Success);\n        }\n\n        match self.client.execute(sql, &amp;[]).await {\n            Ok(actual) =&gt; {\n                if let Some(expected_error) = expected_error {\n                    return Ok(Outcome::UnexpectedPlanSuccess {\n                        expected_error,\n                        location,\n                    });\n                }\n                match expected_rows_affected {\n                    None =&gt; Ok(Outcome::Success),\n                    Some(expected) =&gt; {\n                        if expected != actual {\n                            Ok(Outcome::WrongNumberOfRowsInserted {\n                                expected_count: expected,\n                                actual_count: actual,\n                                location,\n                            })\n                        } else {\n                            Ok(Outcome::Success)\n                        }\n                    }\n                }\n            }\n            Err(error) =&gt; {\n                if let Some(expected_error) = expected_error {\n                    if Regex::new(expected_error)?.is_match(&amp;error.to_string_with_causes()) {\n                        return Ok(Outcome::Success);\n                    }\n                }\n                Ok(Outcome::PlanFailure {\n                    error: anyhow!(error),\n                    location,\n                })\n            }\n        }\n    }\n\n    async fn prepare_query&lt;'r&gt;(\n        &amp;self,\n        sql: &amp;str,",

        "contents": "impl&lt;'a&gt; RunnerInner&lt;'a&gt; {\n    pub async fn start(config: &amp;RunConfig&lt;'a&gt;) -&gt; Result&lt;RunnerInner&lt;'a&gt;, anyhow::Error&gt; {\n        let temp_dir = tempfile::tempdir()?;\n        let scratch_dir = tempfile::tempdir()?;\n        let environment_id = EnvironmentId::for_tests();\n        let (consensus_uri, timestamp_oracle_url): (SensitiveUrl, SensitiveUrl) = {\n            let postgres_url = &amp;config.postgres_url;\n            let prefix = &amp;config.prefix;\n            info!(%postgres_url, \"starting server\");\n            let (client, conn) = Retry::default()\n                .max_tries(5)\n                .retry_async(|_| async {\n                    match tokio_postgres::connect(postgres_url, NoTls).await {\n                        Ok(c) =&gt; Ok(c),\n                        Err(e) =&gt; {\n                            error!(%e, \"failed to connect to postgres\");\n                            Err(e)\n                        }\n                    }\n                })\n                .await?;\n            task::spawn(|| \"sqllogictest_connect\", async move {\n                if let Err(e) = conn.await {\n                    panic!(\"connection error: {}\", e);\n                }\n            });\n            client\n                .batch_execute(&amp;format!(\n                    \"DROP SCHEMA IF EXISTS {prefix}_tsoracle CASCADE;\n                     CREATE SCHEMA IF NOT EXISTS {prefix}_consensus;\n                     CREATE SCHEMA {prefix}_tsoracle;\"\n                ))\n                .await?;\n            (\n                format!(\"{postgres_url}?options=--search_path={prefix}_consensus\")\n                    .parse()\n                    .expect(\"invalid consensus URI\"),\n                format!(\"{postgres_url}?options=--search_path={prefix}_tsoracle\")\n                    .parse()\n                    .expect(\"invalid timestamp oracle URI\"),\n            )\n        };\n\n        let secrets_dir = temp_dir.path().join(\"secrets\");\n        let orchestrator = Arc::new(\n            ProcessOrchestrator::new",

        "contents": "await?;\n        Ok(())\n    }\n\n    async fn run_record&lt;'r&gt;(\n        &amp;mut self,\n        record: &amp;'r Record&lt;'r&gt;,\n        in_transaction: &amp;mut bool,\n    ) -&gt; Result&lt;Outcome&lt;'r&gt;, anyhow::Error&gt; {\n        match &amp;record {\n            Record::Statement {\n                expected_error,\n                rows_affected,\n                sql,\n                location,\n            } =&gt; {\n                if self.auto_transactions &amp;&amp; *in_transaction {\n                    self.client.execute(\"COMMIT\", &amp;[]).await?;\n                    *in_transaction = false;\n                }\n                match self\n                    .run_statement(*expected_error, *rows_affected, sql, location.clone())\n                    .await?\n                {\n                    Outcome::Success =&gt; {\n                        if self.auto_index_tables {\n                            let additional = mutate(sql);\n                            for stmt in additional {\n                                self.client.execute(&amp;stmt, &amp;[]).await?;\n                            }\n                        }\n                        Ok(Outcome::Success)\n                    }\n                    other =&gt; {\n                        if expected_error.is_some() {\n                            Ok(other)\n                        } else {\n                            // If we failed to execute a statement that was supposed to succeed,\n                            // running the rest of the tests in this file will probably cause\n                            // false positives, so just give up on the file entirely.\n                            Ok(Outcome::Bail {\n                                cause: Box::new(other),\n                                location: location.clone(),\n                            })\n                        }\n                    }\n                }\n            }\n            Record::Query {\n                sql",

    {
      "codeBlock": {
        "relativeWorkspacePath": "./src/sqllogictest/src/runner.rs",
        "range": {

            "column": 9
          },
          "endPosition": {
            "line": 1908,
            "column": 48
          }
        },
        "contents": "    }\n\n    async fn run_simple&lt;'r&gt;(\n        &amp;mut self,\n        conn: Option&lt;&amp;'r str&gt;,\n        user: Option&lt;&amp;'r str&gt;,\n        password: Option&lt;&amp;'r str&gt;,\n        sql: &amp;'r str,\n        sort: Sort,\n        output: &amp;'r Output,\n        location: Location,\n    ) -&gt; Result&lt;Outcome&lt;'r&gt;, anyhow::Error&gt; {\n        let actual = match self.get_conn(conn, user, password).await {\n            Ok(client) =&gt; match client.simple_query(sql).await {\n                Ok(result) =&gt; {\n                    let mut rows = Vec::new();\n\n                    for m in result.into_iter() {\n                        match m {\n                            SimpleQueryMessage::Row(row) =&gt; {\n                                let mut s = vec![];\n                                for i in 0..row.len() {\n                                    s.push(row.get(i).unwrap_or(\"NULL\"));\n                                }\n                                rows.push(s.join(\",\"));\n                            }\n                            SimpleQueryMessage::CommandComplete(count) =&gt; {\n                                // This applies any sort on the COMPLETE line as\n                                // well, but we do the same for the expected output.\n                                rows.push(format!(\"COMPLETE {}\", count));\n                            }\n                            SimpleQueryMessage::RowDescription(_) =&gt; {}\n                            _ =&gt; panic!(\"unexpected\"),\n                        }\n                    }\n\n                    if let Sort::Row = sort {\n                        rows.sort();\n                    }\n\n                    Output::Values(rows)\n                }\n                // Errors can contain multiple lines (say if there are details), and rewrite\n                // sticks them each on their own line, so we need to split up the lines here to\n                // each be its own String in the Vec.\n                Err(error) =&gt; Output::Values(\n                    error\n                        .to_string_with_causes()",

        "contents": "            }\n        }\n    }\n\n    let config = RunConfig {\n        stdout: &amp;OutputStream::new(io::stdout(), args.timestamps),\n        stderr: &amp;OutputStream::new(io::stderr(), args.timestamps),\n        verbosity: args.verbosity,\n        postgres_url: args.postgres_url.clone(),\n        prefix: args.prefix.clone(),\n        no_fail: args.no_fail,\n        fail_fast: args.fail_fast,\n        auto_index_tables: args.auto_index_tables,\n        auto_index_selects: args.auto_index_selects,\n        auto_transactions: args.auto_transactions,\n        enable_table_keys: args.enable_table_keys,\n        orchestrator_process_wrapper: args.orchestrator_process_wrapper.clone(),\n        tracing: tracing_args.clone(),\n        tracing_handle,\n        system_parameter_defaults,\n        persist_dir: match tempfile::tempdir() {\n            Ok(t) =&gt; t,\n            Err(e) =&gt; {\n                eprintln!(\"error creating state dir: {e}\");\n                return ExitCode::FAILURE;\n            }\n        },\n        replicas: args.replicas,\n        replica_size: args.replica_size.clone(),\n    };\n\n    if let (Some(shard), Some(shard_count)) = (args.shard, args.shard_count) {\n        if shard != 0 || shard_count != 1 {\n            eprintln!(\"Shard: {}/{}\", shard + 1, shard_count);\n        }\n    }\n\n    if args.rewrite_results {\n        return rewrite(&amp;config, args).await;\n    }\n\n    let mut junit = match args.junit_report {\n        Some(filename) =&gt; match File::create(&amp;filename) {\n            Ok(file) =&gt; Some((file, junit_report::TestSuite::new(\"sqllogictest\"))),\n            Err(err) =&gt; {\n                writeln!(config.stderr, \"creating {}: {}\", filename.display(), err);\n                return ExitCode::FAILURE;\n            }\n        },\n        None =&gt; None,\n    };\n    let mut outcomes = Outcomes::default();\n    let mut runner = Runner::start(&amp;config).await.unwrap();\n    let mut paths = args.paths;\n\n    if let (Some(shard), Some(shard_count)) =",

          }
        },
        "contents": "    async fn prepare_query&lt;'r&gt;(\n        &amp;self,\n        sql: &amp;str,\n        output: &amp;'r Result&lt;QueryOutput&lt;'_&gt;, &amp;'r str&gt;,\n        location: Location,\n        in_transaction: &amp;mut bool,\n    ) -&gt; Result&lt;PrepareQueryOutcome&lt;'r&gt;, anyhow::Error&gt; {\n        // get statement\n        let statements = match mz_sql::parse::parse(sql) {\n            Ok(statements) =&gt; statements,\n            Err(e) =&gt; match output {\n                Ok(_) =&gt; {\n                    return Ok(PrepareQueryOutcome::Outcome(Outcome::ParseFailure {\n                        error: e.into(),\n                        location,\n                    }));\n                }\n                Err(expected_error) =&gt; {\n                    if Regex::new(expected_error)?.is_match(&amp;e.to_string_with_causes()) {\n                        return Ok(PrepareQueryOutcome::Outcome(Outcome::Success));\n                    } else {\n                        return Ok(PrepareQueryOutcome::Outcome(Outcome::ParseFailure {\n                            error: e.into(),\n                            location,\n                        }));\n                    }\n                }\n            },\n        };\n        let statement = match &amp;*statements {\n            [] =&gt; bail!(\"Got zero statements?\"),\n            [statement] =&gt; &amp;statement.ast,\n            _ =&gt; bail!(\"Got multiple statements: {:?}\", statements),\n        };\n        let (is_select, num_attributes) = match statement {\n            Statement::Select(stmt) =&gt; (true, derive_num_attributes(&amp;stmt.query.body)),\n            _ =&gt; (false, None),\n        };\n\n        match output {\n            Ok(_) =&gt;",
        "signatures": {},
        "detailedLines": [
          {
            "text": "    async fn prepare_query&lt;'r&gt;(",
            "lineNumber": 1477
          },

            "lineNumber": 1480
          },

            "text": "        location: Location,",
            "lineNumber": 1481
          },
          {
            "text": "        in_transaction: &amp;mut bool,",
            "lineNumber": 1482

          {
            "text": "    ) -&gt; Result&lt;PrepareQueryOutcome&lt;'r&gt;, anyhow::Error&gt; {",
            "lineNumber": 1483
          },
          {
            "text": "        // get statement",
            "lineNumber": 1484
          },

            "text": "        let statements = match mz_sql::parse::parse(sql) {",
            "lineNumber": 1485
          },

            "text": "            Ok(statements) =&gt; statements,",
            "lineNumber": 1486

          },
          "endPosition": {
            "line": 80

        },
        "contents": "#[derive(clap::Parser)]\nstruct Args {\n    /// Increase verbosity.\n    ///\n    /// If specified once, print summary for each source file.\n    /// If specified twice, also show descriptions of each error.\n    /// If specified thrice, also print each query before it is executed.\n    #[clap(short = 'v', long = \"verbose\", action = ArgAction::Count)]\n    verbosity: u8,\n    /// Don't exit with a failing code if not all queries are successful.\n    #[clap(long)]\n    no_fail: bool,\n    /// Prefix every line of output with the current time.\n    #[clap(long)]\n    timestamps: bool,\n    /// Rewrite expected output based on actual output.\n    #[clap(long)]\n    rewrite_results: bool,\n    /// Generate a JUnit-compatible XML report to the specified file.\n    #[clap(long, value_name = \"FILE\")]\n    junit_report: Option&lt;PathBuf&gt;,\n    /// PostgreSQL connection URL to use for &#96;persist&#96; consensus.\n    #[clap(long)]\n    postgres_url: String,\n    /// PostgreSQL prefix for this SLT run\n    #[clap(long, default_value = \"sqllogictest\")]\n    prefix: String,\n    /// Path to sqllogictest script to run.\n    #[clap(value_name = \"PATH\", required = true)]\n    paths: Vec&lt;String&gt;,\n    /// Stop on first failure.\n    #[clap(long)]\n    fail_fast: bool,\n    /// Inject &#96;CREATE INDEX&#96; after all &#96;CREATE TABLE&#96; statements.\n    #[clap(long)]\n    auto_index_tables: bool,\n    /// Inject &#96;CREATE VIEW &lt;view_name&gt; AS &lt;select_query&gt;&#96; and &#96;CREATE DEFAULT INDEX ON &lt;view_name&gt; ...&#96;\n    /// to redundantly execute a given &#96;SELECT&#96; query and contrast outcomes.\n    #[clap(long)]\n    auto_index_selects: bool,\n    /// Inject &#96;BEGIN&#96; and &#96;COMMIT&#96; to create longer running transactions for faster testing of the\n    /// ported SQLite SLT files. Does not work generally, so don't use it for other tests.\n    #[clap(long)]\n    auto_transactions: bool,\n    /// Inject &#96;ALTER SYSTEM SET unsafe_enable_table_keys = true&#96; before running the SLT file.\n    #[clap(long)]\n    enable_table_keys: bool,\n    /// Divide the test files into shards and run only the test files in this shard.",

        "contents": "        view_order_by.push(OrderByExpr {\n            expr: view_expr,\n            asc: order_by_expr.asc,\n            nulls_last: order_by_expr.nulls_last,\n        });\n    }\n    (view_order_by, extra_columns)\n}\n\n/// Returns extra statements to execute after &#96;stmt&#96; is executed.\nfn mutate(sql: &amp;str) -&gt; Vec&lt;String&gt; {\n    let stmts = parser::parse_statements(sql).unwrap_or_default();\n    let mut additional = Vec::new();\n    for stmt in stmts {\n        match stmt.ast {\n            AstStatement::CreateTable(stmt) =&gt; additional.push(\n                // CREATE TABLE -&gt; CREATE INDEX. Specify all columns manually in case CREATE\n                // DEFAULT INDEX ever goes away.\n                AstStatement::&lt;Raw&gt;::CreateIndex(CreateIndexStatement {\n                    name: None,\n                    in_cluster: None,\n                    on_name: RawItemName::Name(stmt.name.clone()),\n                    key_parts: Some(\n                        stmt.columns\n                            .iter()\n                            .map(|def| Expr::Identifier(vec![def.name.clone()]))\n                            .collect(),\n                    ),\n                    with_options: Vec::new(),\n                    if_not_exists: false,\n                })\n                .to_ast_string_stable(),\n            ),\n            _ =&gt; {}\n        }\n    }\n    additional\n}\n\n#[mz_ore::test]\n#[cfg_attr(miri, ignore)] // unsupported operation: can't call foreign function &#96;rust_psm_stack_pointer&#96; on OS &#96;linux&#96;\nfn test_generate_view_sql() {\n    let uuid =",

        "contents": "[package]\nname = \"mz-sqllogictest\"\ndescription = \"A driver for sqllogictest, a SQL correctness testing framework.\"\nversion = \"0.0.1\"\nedition.workspace = true\nrust-version.workspace = true\npublish = false\n\n[lints]\nworkspace = true\n\n[dependencies]\nanyhow = \"1.0.101\"\nbytes = \"1.11.1\"\nchrono = { version = \"0.4.39\", default-features = false, features = [\"std\"] }\nclap = { version = \"4.5.23\", features = [\"derive\"] }\ndec = \"0.4.8\"\nfallible-iterator = \"0.2.0\"\nfutures = \"0.3.31\"\nitertools = \"0.14.0\"\njunit-report = \"0.8.3\"\nmaplit = \"1.0.2\"\nmd-5 = \"0.10.6\"\nmz-adapter-types = { path = \"../adapter-types\" }\nmz-authenticator = { path = \"../authenticator\", default-features = false }\nmz-build-info = { path = \"../build-info\" }\nmz-catalog = { path = \"../catalog\" }\nmz-controller = { path = \"../controller\" }\nmz-dyncfgs = { path = \"../dyncfgs\" }\nmz-environmentd = { path = \"../environmentd\", default-features = false }\nmz-license-keys = { path = \"../license-keys\" }\nmz-ore = { path = \"../ore\", features = [\"async\", \"panic\", \"tracing\"] }\nmz-orchestrator = { path = \"../orchestrator\" }\nmz-orchestrator-process = { path = \"../orchestrator-process\" }\nmz-orchestrator-tracing = { path = \"../orchestrator-tracing\" }\nmz-persist-client = { path = \"../persist-client\" }\nmz-pgrepr = { path = \"../pgrepr\" }\nmz-repr = { path = \"../repr\" }\nmz-secrets = { path = \"../secrets\" }\nmz-server-core = { path = \"../server-core\" }\nmz-service = { path = \"../service\" }\nmz-sql = { path = \"../sql\" }\nmz-sql-parser = { path = \"../sql-parser\" }\nmz-storage-types = { path = \"../storage-types\" }\nmz-tracing = { path = \"../tracing\" }\npostgres-protocol = { version = \"0.6.9\" }\nregex = \"1.12.2\"\nreqwest = { version = \"0.12.28\", features = [\"json\"] }\nshell-words = \"1.1.1\"\nserde_json = \"1.0.149\"\ntempfile = \"3.23.0\"\ntime = \"0.3.17\"\ntracing = \"0.1.44\"\ntokio = \"1.48.0\"\ntokio-postgres = { version = \"0.7.15\", features = [\n    \"with-chrono-0_4\",\n    \"with-uuid-1\",\n    \"with-serde_json-1\",\n] }\ntokio-stream = { version = \"0.1.18\", features = [\"net\"] }\ntower-http = { version = \"0.6.7\", features = [\"cors\"] }\nuuid = \"1.19.0\"",

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

So in sqllogictest:
- "copy table_name path/to/file" is a directive that loads from a TSV file and runs COPY table_name FROM STDIN with that file's contents.
- "simple" records can run arbitrary SQL including "COPY atest2 FROM stdin" but the comment says "Can't test COPY with SLT?" - so when you run "COPY x FROM stdin" as a statement, what happens? The simple directive just sends the SQL - it doesn't send CopyData. So for COPY FROM stdin you need to send the SQL, then send the data. The pgtest format does that with CopyData, CopyDone.

So for sqllogictest we have two options:
1. Use the "copy" directive with a **file** that contains invalid range data. So we'd create a small .tsv (or .data) file with one line "[7,3)" and a table with one int4range column, then "copy t copy_invalid_range.data" - and we'd expect an error. The runner does copy_in and sends the file contents - so if the file has "[7,3)\n" that would trigger the bug. We need to check what format the copy directive expects - it says TSV. So the file would be just one column: the range. So a one-line file with "[7,3)" and we need to expect an error. But the Record::Copy doesn't have an expected_error - it just does the copy and expects success. So we'd need a different approach.

2. Use a "statement error" with COPY - but COPY FROM stdin in a statement form doesn't include the data in the SQL. So we can't do "statement error ... COPY t FROM STDIN" and then have the data in the next line - the statement would be just "COPY t FROM STDIN" and the runner would execute it, and then... the connection would be waiting for CopyData. So the way sqllogictest works, "statement" sends one SQL and gets one response. So we can't easily do COPY FROM STDIN with inline data in sqllogictest.

Let me look at the copy directive again. Record::Copy { table_name, tsv_path } - it reads the file and sends it. So if we have a test that does:
- statement ok: CREATE TABLE t(r int4range);
- copy t somefile.data

And somefile.data contains "[7,3)\n", then when the runner runs the copy it will send that and the server will try to decode it. Currently that panics. So we need the copy directive to support an expected error. Looking at the runner code again:

```rust
Record::Copy {
    table_name,
    tsv_path,
} => {
    let tsv = tokio::fs::read(tsv_path).await?;
    let copy = self
        .client
        .copy_in(&*format!("COPY {} FROM STDIN", table_name))
        .await?;
    tokio::pin!(copy);
    copy.send(bytes::Bytes::from(tsv)).await?;
    copy.finish().await?;
    Ok(Outcome::Success)
}
```

So it doesn't support expecting an error. We have two paths:

A) Add a new record type or variant like "copy error" that expects an error, and a data file. That would require parser and runner changes.

B) Use pgtest (.pt) instead - the user said "ideally a sqllogictest". So maybe we can do a minimal sqllogictest that uses the existing "copy" directive but we need to extend the format to allow expected error. Let me check if there's a "statement error" style for copy... There isn't in the parser.

C) Put the test in test/pgtest/ as a .pt file that sends COPY ... CopyData "[7,3)\n" CopyDone and expects ErrorResponse with the range error message. That would repro the bug (currently panic) and once we fix it, we'd expect the error. So the test would be: run COPY t FROM STDIN, send "[7,3)\n", CopyDone, then expect ErrorResponse with "range lower bound must be less than or equal to range upper bound". That's a good repro. But the user asked for sqllogictest.

Let me check the sqllogictest copy directive again - maybe we can have a "copy" that points to a file and then the next record is "query error" or something? No - each record is independent. So the only way in pure sqllogictest to test COPY with invalid data would be to extend the parser to support something like:

copy t invalid_range.data
error range lower bound must be less than or equal to range upper bound

That would be a new record type or an optional error on the copy record. Let me look at how "statement error" is parsed.
</details></think>

---

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

So the "copy" directive is "copy table_name path" and there's no "copy error" variant. The copy directive always expects success. So to add a test that reproduces the bug in sqllogictest we have two options:

1. **Extend sqllogictest** to support "copy error" or "copy ... error <message>" so we can have a copy that expects an error. That's more invasive.

2. **Use a pgtest** for the repro - it's the right format for COPY with CopyData. Then we could also add a note in the plan that the regression test is in pgtest.

3. **Use sqllogictest with the existing "copy" directive** - if we run "copy t invalid.data" and invalid.data contains "[7,3)", the server will currently **panic** (so the test would fail/crash). Once we fix the bug, the server would return an error to the client, and the copy_in would fail - but the runner would get an error from copy.finish() or copy.send(). So the test would still fail because the runner expects Ok(Outcome::Success). So we need the runner to support expected error for copy, or we need to use pgtest.

I'll go with adding a **pgtest** that reproduces the bug (COPY with invalid range, expect error). That's the format that already supports COPY with CopyData. I'll put it in test/pgtest/ or test/pgtest-mz/. Then I'll also add a **sqllogictest** that documents the INSERT behavior (invalid range gives a proper error) - that already works. And we could add a new sqllogictest file that uses the "copy" directive with a data file and extend the parser/runner to support "copy error <message>" so the test can expect an error. That would be the ideal "slt" test the user asked for.

Actually re-reading the user message: "lets write a test that repros the problem, ideally a sqllogictest". So they want to repro the problem. The problem is COPY with invalid range panics. So:
- A test that runs COPY with invalid range and currently would panic (or after fix, would get an error).
- Ideally in sqllogictest.

The minimal way to get that in sqllogictest is to add support for expecting an error on copy. So we'd have something like:

  statement ok
  CREATE TABLE t(r int4range);

  copy t copy_invalid_range.data
  error range lower bound must be less than or equal to range upper bound

---

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

For "copy", we only get one line. To add optional "error <message>", we'd need to read the next line. So after parsing "copy table path", we could split_at the next line - if it starts with "error " then take the rest as expected_error. But that would consume the next line. So the format would be:

copy t path/to/data.data
error range lower bound must be less than or equal to range upper bound

And the next record would start after a blank line. So when we parse "copy", we need to optionally consume one more line. Let me check - after we split_at(COMMENT_AND_LINE_REGEX) we get first_line and we've consumed up to and including the newline. So the next "first_line" in the next parse_record would be "error ...". So we can't do it in the same parse_record without explicitly reading ahead. So in the "copy" branch we need to: parse table and path, then check if there's more content and the next line (after trimming) starts with "error "; if so, consume that line and use the rest as expected_error. So we need to look at self.contents - after the first_line we consumed, what's next? We have split_at which consumes. So after "copy t path\n", contents might be "error range...\n\n" or "----" or next record. So we need to split_at again to get the next line, and if it starts with "error " we take it and set expected_error. So:

"copy" => {
    let table_name = ...;
    let tsv_path = ...;
    let expected_error = None;
    // Peek at next line
    if let Some(next_line_match) = COMMENT_AND_LINE_REGEX.find(self.contents) {
        let next_line = self.contents[..next_line_match.start()].trim();
        if next_line.starts_with("error ") {
            let msg = next_line["error ".len()..].trim();
            self.consume(next_line_match.end()); // consume the line
            expected_error = Some(msg); // but we need &'a str - we consumed so we can't get &'a str from contents that we're about to consume
        }
    }
    Ok(Record::Copy { table_name, tsv_path, expected_error })
}

The problem is expected_error needs to be &'a str pointing into the original contents. If we consume the line, that memory is still there in the original buffer - no wait, we're doing self.contents = &self.contents[upto..], so we're reborrowing. So after consume(), the "error ..." line is no longer in self.contents. So we'd need to copy the message to get a &'a str, but we can't - we'd need to use a String or the message would need to be stored elsewhere. Actually in the parser, all the &'a str point into the original contents. So if we consume the "error ..." line, we lose the reference. So we have two options: (1) Don't consume the "error" line in the copy branch - just peek and record the slice. The slice would be into self.contents. So we get the start and end of the line, and we use &self.contents[start..end] for the message. But then that line is still in contents and the next parse_record will see "error ..." as the first line and bail with "Unexpected start of record". So we must consume it. So we have to consume and then we lose the &'a str. So we need to either (a) store the error message in an owned String in Record (but Record<'a> is all borrowed), or (b) consume only after we've recorded the pointer. So: we find the next line, get the message as &self.contents[..next_line_match.start()] trimmed and "error ".len() stripped - that gives us a &str that is valid as long as contents is valid. Then we call consume(next_line_match.end()). Now contents is advanced, but our &str pointed into the old contents. In Rust, that would be a dangling reference because we advanced self.contents. So we can't do that. So the only way is to have the "error" line be part of the same "first_line" or we need an owned String. Let me check Record - it uses 'a everywhere. So we'd need to add Cow<'a, str> or String for the error. The simplest is Option<String> for expected_error so we own the message. Then we'd need to change Record::Copy to have expected_error: Option<String>. But that might require cloning. Alternatively, we could use a different format: "copy table path error" on the same line with the error message, but that's awkward for long messages. Let me use Option<String> for the expected error so we can consume the line and store the message.
</details></think>

Wait, I made an error. The "copy" directive is one line: "copy table path". So words would be ["copy", "table", "path"] and there's no "error" on that line. So the optional "error" is on the *next* line. So we should only look at the next line for "error ...". Let me simplify: don't try to parse "error" from the first line; only peek at the next line. So:

"copy" => {
    let table_name = words.next()...;
    let tsv_path = words.next()...;
    let expected_error = None;
    if let Some(m) = COMMENT_AND_LINE_REGEX.find(self.contents) {
        let next_line = self.contents[..m.start()].trim();
        if next_line.starts_with("error ") {
            self.consume(m.end());
            expected_error = Some(next_line["error ".len()..].trim().to_string());
        }
    }
    Ok(Record::Copy { table_name, tsv_path, expected_error })
}
</details></think>

- Runs `COPY copy_range_t FROM STDIN` and sends one row: `[7,3)` (invalid: lower 7 > upper 3)
- Expects an `ErrorResponse`

**Behavior:** With the current bug, the server panics during COPY and the test fails. After the fix (returning a `Result` from `into_datum` and handling it in COPY), the server will send an `ErrorResponse` instead of panicking and the test will pass.

No sqllogictest or parser changes were made; the repro is only in pgtest. Run it with your usual pgtest runner (e.g. the same way you run `copy-from.pt`).

_**User**_

What is a pgtest, why can't it be a sqllogictest, slt?

---

_**Agent (model default, mode Agent)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

The user wants to understand what pgtest is and why we used it instead of sqllogictest (slt) for the COPY invalid-range repro test.
</details></think>

---


**What is pgtest?**

**pgtest** is Materialize’s **protocol-level** test format. A `.pt` file is a script of messages on the PostgreSQL wire protocol: you send exact bytes (e.g. `Query`, `Parse`, `Bind`, `Execute`, `CopyData`, `CopyDone`, `Sync`) and assert on the responses (`CopyIn`, `ErrorResponse`, `ReadyForQuery`, etc.). The test runner talks to the server over the real PostgreSQL protocol, so it can do multi-step flows like “send COPY, then stream CopyData, then CopyDone” and then check that the server sends back an `ErrorResponse` (or whatever you expect).

So pgtest is **low-level**: you’re testing the protocol and server behavior message-by-message.

---

**Why couldn’t this be sqllogictest (slt)?**

**sqllogictest** is a **SQL-level** test format. An `.slt` file is a list of **records**: each record is either a “statement” (run this SQL, expect success or a specific error) or a “query” (run this SQL, expect this result set). The runner sends **one** statement or query and gets **one** response. There is no notion of “send COPY, then send copy data, then send CopyDone” in a single record; COPY FROM STDIN is inherently a **multi-message** operation (COPY command → CopyData stream → CopyDone).

Slt does have a **`copy` directive** (`copy <table> <path>`), which:

- Sends `COPY <table> FROM STDIN`
- Reads a file and sends its contents as CopyData
- Sends CopyDone

So you *can* trigger COPY in slt. The limitation is **what happens when COPY fails**. With the current slt runner, a `copy` record is always expected to **succeed**. If the server returns an error (e.g. invalid range), the runner gets an error from `copy.finish()` and treats the whole test as failed—there’s no way in the format to say “this copy is *expected* to fail with such-and-such error.” So:

- **Before the fix:** COPY with `[7,3)` → server **panics** → test fails (good: we see the bug).
- **After the fix:** COPY with `[7,3)` → server returns an **error** → runner still treats it as failure because it doesn’t expect an error for `copy`.

To make the test “pass after fix” in slt, we’d need to extend the format (e.g. an optional “this copy should fail with error …”) and the runner. We didn’t do that so we could keep the repro test minimal.

---

**Summary**

| | pgtest (.pt) | sqllogictest (.slt) |
|---|--------------|---------------------|
| **Level** | Protocol (messages) | SQL (statements/queries) |
| **COPY** | You send Query + CopyData + CopyDone and can assert on ErrorResponse | `copy table path` sends COPY + file; no way to say “expect error” |
| **This repro** | Natural fit: one script that does COPY with bad data and expects ErrorResponse. Fails (panic) now, passes after fix. | Can trigger the same COPY via `copy table file`, but without “expected error” support the test would still fail after the fix. |

So the repro is in pgtest because it needs to **expect an error from COPY**, and slt’s `copy` directive doesn’t support that yet. It could be an slt test if we added something like “copy table path” with an optional “error &lt;substring&gt;” and updated the runner to treat that as success when COPY fails with a matching message.

              "matches": [
                {
                  "lineNumber": 10,
                  "content": "//! pgtest is a Postgres wire protocol tester using datadriven test files. It"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 11,
                  "content": "//! can be used to send [specific",
                  "isContextLine": true
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 12,
                  "content": "//! messages](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/current/protocol-message-formats.html)",
                  "isContextLine": true
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 48,
                  "content": "//! &#96;&#96;&#96;pgtest"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 49,
                  "content": "//! send",
                  "isContextLine": true
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 50,
                  "content": "//! Parse {\"query\": \"SELECT $1::text, 1 + $2::int4\"}",
                  "isContextLine": true
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 69,
                  "content": "//! a postgres server. Use the &#96;pgtest-mz&#96; directory if our output differs"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 70,
                  "content": "//! incompatibly from postgres. Write your test, excluding any lines after the",
                  "isContextLine": true
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 71,
                  "content": "//! &#96;----&#96; of the &#96;until&#96; directive. For example:",
                  "isContextLine": true
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 72,
                  "content": "//! &#96;&#96;&#96;pgtest"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 73,
                  "content": "//! send",
                  "isContextLine": true
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 74,
                  "content": "//! Query {\"query\": \"SELECT 1\"}",
                  "isContextLine": true
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 81,
                  "content": "//! Then run the pgtest binary, enabling rewrites and pointing it at postgres:"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 82,
                  "content": "//! &#96;&#96;&#96;shell",
                  "isContextLine": true
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 83,
                  "content": "//! REWRITE=1 cargo run --bin mz-pgtest -- test/pgtest/test.pt --addr localhost:5432 --user postgres"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 84,
                  "content": "//! &#96;&#96;&#96;",
                  "isContextLine": true
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 85,
                  "content": "//! This will generate the expected output for the &#96;until&#96; directive. Now rerun",
                  "isContextLine": true
                },

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

I found it. To run pgtest:
1. As integration tests from environmentd: `cargo test -p mz-environmentd test_pgtest_*` (or nextest with the filter in .config/nextest.toml). The test for our new file would need to be added to src/environmentd/tests/pgwire.rs as `test_pgtest_copy_from_range`.
2. Or run the pgtest binary: `cargo run --bin mz-pgtest -- test/pgtest/copy-from-range.pt` (and optionally with --addr and --user for Postgres).

I need to:
1. Add a test_pgtest_copy_from_range function in environmentd/tests/pgwire.rs that runs our .pt file.
2. Update copy-from-range.pt to add positive cases: valid ranges that COPY successfully, then SELECT to verify. I'll add a few valid rows like [1,10), [0,0), empty, etc. before the invalid case.
</details></think>

                  "lineNumber": 6,

                  "lineNumber": 8,

                },

                },

                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 12,

                  "isContextLine": true
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 13,
                  "isContextLine": true
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 14,

---


Rust isn’t available in this environment, so the test wasn’t run here. Here’s what was added and how to run it.

---

**How to run the pgtest**

1. **As an integration test (Materialize started for you):**
   ```bash
   cargo test -p mz-environmentd test_pgtest_copy_from_range
   ```
   Or with nextest:
   ```bash
   cargo nextest run -p mz-environmentd test_pgtest_copy_from_range
   ```

2. **Using the pgtest binary** (you must have Materialize or Postgres listening):
   ```bash
   cargo run --bin mz-pgtest -- test/pgtest/copy-from-range.pt --addr localhost:6875 --user materialize
   ```
   To regenerate the expected output (e.g. after changing the test or fixing the bug):
   ```bash
   REWRITE=1 cargo run --bin mz-pgtest -- test/pgtest/copy-from-range.pt --addr localhost:6875 --user materialize
   ```

---

**Changes made**

1. **`src/environmentd/tests/pgwire.rs`**  
   - Registered the new test: `test_pgtest_copy_from_range` so it runs with the other pgtest integration tests.

2. **`test/pgtest/copy-from-range.pt`**  
   - **Positive cases:**  
     - COPY one valid row `[1,10)`.  
     - COPY three more: `[0,0)`, `empty`, `(,)`.  
     - SELECT to verify: expect 4 rows (ordered as in the file: empty, (,), [0,0), [1,10)).  
   - **Negative case (unchanged):**  
     - COPY `[7,3)` and expect `ErrorResponse`.

If the SELECT expectation doesn’t match your build (e.g. different range text format), run the test once and, if it fails only on the expected output, use `REWRITE=1` with the pgtest binary to update the `.pt` file.

---

_**User**_

@/Users/michael.abebe/.cursor/projects/Users-michael-abebe-Code-materialize/terminals/1.txt:27-44 

---

_**Agent (model default, mode Agent)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

The user is saying two things:

1. The test failed - but the failure is **not** the COPY panic we wanted to reproduce. The failure is: `Failed to start test Server: METADATA_BACKEND_URL environment variable is not set`. So the test is failing because the test harness can't start the Materialize server (missing env var), not because of the invalid range bug.

2. They removed the negative test case from the pgtest file, but the test is still failing - and they think the build might not be updating / the test might not be picking up the file change.

So there are two issues:
- **Env var**: The pgtest integration tests in environmentd need METADATA_BACKEND_URL to be set to start the server. Other pgtest tests would have the same requirement - so how do people normally run these? Maybe there's a script or CI that sets it. I should check how other tests set this up.
- **Stale build / file not used**: The .pt files are not compiled into the binary - they're read at runtime from the path `../../test/pgtest/copy-from-range.pt` relative to where the test runs. So when you run from the workspace root, the path is correct. The test binary doesn't "build" the .pt file in. So if they edited the .pt file and re-ran the test, it should read the updated file. Unless: (a) they're running from a different cwd, (b) the test is cached and not re-running, (c) something else. Actually re-reading again - "I don't think it is updating the build" - they might mean the Rust build isn't being triggered when they change the .pt file. That's expected: changing a .pt file doesn't cause cargo to recompile. So when they run `cargo test test_pgtest_copy_from_range` again, it uses the same binary but when the test runs it opens the .pt file fresh. So the updated .pt (without the negative case) should be read. Unless the test fails before it gets to read the file - which is exactly what's happening. The test fails at "Failed to start test Server" - so it never gets to the point of reading the .pt file and running the COPY. So the test is failing at environment setup, not at the actual pgtest content. So the reason "the test is still failing" after removing the negative case is simply that the test never gets to run the pgtest - it fails earlier when trying to start the server.

So I should:
1. Explain that the current failure is METADATA_BACKEND_URL / server startup, not the COPY bug.
2. Tell them how to set METADATA_BACKEND_URL (or run the test in a way that sets it - e.g. from the Makefile or mzcompose).
3. Clarify that .pt files are read at test runtime, so no recompile is needed; once the server starts, their edit (no negative case) would be used.
</details></think>

---


Identifying two separate issues:

1) Why the test is "still failing" after removing the negative case  
The failure is happening before the pgtest runs:

```
Failed to start test Server: METADATA_BACKEND_URL environment variable is not set
```

So the environmentd test harness can't start Materialize; it never reads or runs the .pt file. Removing the negative case doesn't change that.

2) Build / .pt updates  
.pt files aren't compiled in — they're read at runtime from the path in the test. Changing a .pt doesn't trigger a recompile; the same binary will use the updated file when the test runs. No recompile is needed for .pt edits.

Checking how METADATA_BACKEND_URL is set for these tests:

---


Here’s what’s going on and how to run the test.

---

**1. Why it’s “still failing” after removing the negative case**

The failure is from **environment setup**, not from the pgtest steps themselves:

```text
Failed to start test Server: METADATA_BACKEND_URL environment variable is not set
```

The test fails when starting the Materialize server (in `TestHarness::start_blocking()`). It never gets to open or run the `.pt` file, so changing the negative case doesn’t affect this.

---

**2. “Build not updating” / .pt changes**

The `.pt` file is **not** part of the Rust build. It’s read **when the test runs** from:

`../../test/pgtest/copy-from-range.pt`

So:

- Editing the `.pt` file does **not** trigger a recompile (and doesn’t need to).
- The same binary will use the **current** contents of the file on the next run.
- Once the server actually starts, your version of the file (with or without the negative case) is what gets run.

---

**3. How to run the pgtest**

These integration tests expect a real metadata backend, so you must set `METADATA_BACKEND_URL` to a Postgres (or Cockroach) URL. For example:

```bash
# With a local Postgres (or use the URL your team/CI uses)
export METADATA_BACKEND_URL="postgres://user:pass@localhost:5432/postgres"
cargo test -p mz-environmentd test_pgtest_copy_from_range
```

Or run the same test via the project’s CI / mzcompose setup, which already sets `METADATA_BACKEND_URL` (see e.g. `ci/test/cargo-test/mzcompose.py`).

A short comment was added at the top of `test/pgtest/copy-from-range.pt` that mentions:

- The run command
- The need for `METADATA_BACKEND_URL`
- That the `.pt` file is read at runtime so no recompile is needed when editing it

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

The user says METADATA_BACKEND_URL is set to `postgres://root@localhost:26257` but the test still fails with "METADATA_BACKEND_URL environment variable is not set". 

This could be a few things:
1. **Cargo/test runner doesn't inherit the env var** - When you run `cargo test`, it spawns the test process. Sometimes env vars from the shell are inherited, sometimes they're not depending on how the test is invoked (e.g. nextest, or running from IDE might not have the same environment).
2. **Different process** - The test might be spawning a subprocess (e.g. the actual server) that doesn't get the same environment.
3. **Case sensitivity** - Unlikely; we're using METADATA_BACKEND_URL in code.
4. **Test runs in a different context** - e.g. if they run via `cargo nextest run` or from an IDE, the working directory or environment might be different.

Let me check - in test_util.rs we have `env::var("METADATA_BACKEND_URL")`. So it's reading from the process environment. If the user runs `cargo test` from a shell where they've exported METADATA_BACKEND_URL, it should be passed to the test process. Unless:
- They're running the test from an IDE (Cursor, etc.) which might not inherit the shell's environment
- They run `cargo test` in a subshell or a way that doesn't have the var
- nextest or another runner might spawn workers that don't get the env

I should suggest they explicitly pass the var when running the test to rule out inheritance issues, and also check if they're running from an IDE.
</details></think>

---


If `echo $METADATA_BACKEND_URL` shows a value in your shell but the test still says it’s not set, the test process usually isn’t seeing that variable. Common causes:

1. **Running from an IDE (e.g. Cursor)**  
   The test process is started by the IDE, which often doesn’t load your shell profile or your current terminal’s env. So `METADATA_BACKEND_URL` may be set in the terminal but not in the process that runs the test.

2. **Running via nextest or another runner**  
   Some runners spawn worker processes that might not inherit the full environment.

**Try passing the variable explicitly when you run the test:**

```bash
METADATA_BACKEND_URL="postgres://root@localhost:26257" cargo test -p mz-environmentd test_pgtest_copy_from_range
```

Or export and run in the same command:

```bash
export METADATA_BACKEND_URL="postgres://root@localhost:26257"
cargo test -p mz-environmentd test_pgtest_copy_from_range
```

Run that from the same terminal where you’d normally run `cargo test` (not from the IDE’s “Run test”/“Debug test” UI). If it passes when you do that, the failure when using “Run test” in Cursor is due to the IDE not having `METADATA_BACKEND_URL` in its environment.

**To make it work from the IDE**, set the env var in the IDE’s run configuration (e.g. add `METADATA_BACKEND_URL=postgres://root@localhost:26257` to the test’s environment in Cursor/VS Code) or ensure your IDE is started from a shell where `METADATA_BACKEND_URL` is already exported.

The test is now running (METADATA_BACKEND_URL is working). The failure is a mismatch in the expected vs actual SELECT output:

Expected row 3: `[0,0)` 
Actual row 3: `empty`

So the range `[0,0)` is being displayed as `empty` in Materialize. That makes sense - in range semantics, `[0,0)` is a half-open interval that contains no points (lower bound 0 inclusive, upper bound 0 exclusive), so it's the empty range. So Materialize (correctly) displays it as "empty".

I need to update the test's expected output: change the third DataRow from `[0,0)` to `empty`. And we'll have two "empty" rows now - one from the literal "empty" we inserted and one from "[0,0)". The order might be "empty", "empty", "(,)", "[1,10)" or the two empties could be in a different order. The actual output shows:
- empty
- empty  
- (,)
- [1,10)

So we need to update the expected section to match: two DataRows with "empty", then "(,)", then "[1,10)".
</details></think>

In Rust, you can't have both value.rs and value/mod.rs - value.rs would be the module and value/ would be... actually if you have value.rs it's the module content; the value/ directory would only be used if value.rs had `mod interval` etc and the compiler looks for value/interval.rs. So value.rs can have `mod interval;` and that loads value/interval.rs. So the structure is value.rs (main module file) with submodules in value/*.rs. Summary of what was added:

**1. `src/pgrepr/src/value/error.rs`** (new file)

- **`IntoDatumError`** enum with:
  - `Range(InvalidRangeError)` – invalid range (e.g. misordered bounds)
  - `Array(InvalidArrayError)` – invalid array (e.g. wrong cardinality, too many dimensions)
- **`Display`** – delegates to the inner error’s `Display`
- **`Error`** – implements `source()` by returning the inner error
- **`From<InvalidRangeError>`** and **`From<InvalidArrayError>`** – so you can use `?` and `.into()`

**2. `src/pgrepr/src/value.rs`**

- Added `pub mod error;` with the other value submodules.

**3. `src/pgrepr/src/lib.rs`**

- Re-export: `pub use value::error::IntoDatumError;` so the crate exposes `mz_pgrepr::IntoDatumError`.

Lints are clean. I couldn’t run `cargo check` in this environment (no rustup), so run `cargo check -p mz-pgrepr` locally to confirm. The type is defined and exported but not used yet; next step is to change `into_datum` to return `Result<Datum<'a>, IntoDatumError>` and update call sites.

The user wants me to change `into_datum` to return `Result<Datum<'a>, IntoDatumError>` instead of `Datum<'a>`. I need to:

4. For Array: replace `.unwrap()` on try_push_array with `?`
5. For recursive calls (Array, List, Map, Range elements): the inner `into_datum` will now return Result, so I need to handle that - collect with Result or use ? in a loop

The tricky part is:
- Array: elements.into_iter().map(|element| element.into_datum(...)) - now into_datum returns Result, so we need something like .collect::<Result<Vec<_>, _>>()? or use a loop
- List: same
- Map: same for values
- Range: range.into_bounds(|elem| elem.into_datum(...)) - the closure would need to return Result<Datum, E>, but into_bounds expects Fn(D) -> O. So we can't pass a closure that returns Result directly. We need to build the range manually: get the two bounds from the pgrepr range, call into_datum on each with ?, then construct the repr Range and push_range(range)?.

---

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

So we have Range<Box<Value>> in pgrepr. It has .inner: Option<RangeInner<Box<Value>>> and RangeInner has lower and upper, each RangeBound with .inclusive and .bound: Option<Box<Value>>. So to build the repr Range fallibly I need to:
1. For each bound (lower, upper), if bound is Some(elem), call elem.into_datum(buf, elem_pg_type)? to get Datum
2. Build RangeLowerBound and RangeUpperBound with those datums
3. Range::new(Some((lower, upper)))
4. buf.make_datum(|packer| packer.push_range(range)?) - but make_datum might not accept a closure that returns Result. Let me check make_datum.
</details></think>

---

<tool-use data-tool-type="read" data-tool-name="read_file_v2">
<details><summary>Tool use: **read_file_v2** • Read file: /Users/michael.abebe/Code/materialize/src/repr/src/adt/range.rs</summary>

</details>
</tool-use>

---

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

make_datum takes F: FnOnce(&mut RowPacker) - so the closure doesn't return a value (or returns ()). So we can't use make_datum with a closure that returns Result. We need to:
1. Build the range outside of make_datum (so we can use ?)
2. Then call make_datum with a closure that just does packer.push_range(range)? - but that returns Result, and make_datum expects FnOnce that doesn't return Result. So we have two options:
   a) Build the range and the full datum outside: create a Row with the range pushed, then push that row to the arena. So we'd do something like: let range = ...?; let row = Row::default(); row.packer().push_range(range)?; Ok(buf.push_unary_row(row)) - but push_unary_row is on RowArena. Let me check RowArena for push_unary_row.

So we can't use make_datum for the Range case if push_range can fail. We have to build the datum manually. Let me check - push_unary_row takes a Row. So we could: let mut row = Row::default(); row.packer().push_range(range)?; Ok(buf.push_unary_row(row)). There's try_make_datum! So we can use buf.try_make_datum(|packer| packer.push_range(range)) for the Range case - that returns Result<Datum, E> where E is the error from push_range. So we need to map the error to IntoDatumError. push_range returns Result<(), InvalidRangeError>, and we have From<InvalidRangeError> for IntoDatumError, so we can do .map_err(Into::into) to get Result<(), IntoDatumError>. So:

Value::Range(range) => {
  let elem_pg_type = ...;
  let range = ... build range with fallible into_datum on bounds ...?;
  buf.try_make_datum(|packer| packer.push_range(range).map_err(Into::into))?
}

So we need to build the range. The pgrepr type is Range<Box<Value>>. It has .inner: Option<RangeInner<Box<Value>>>. RangeInner has lower: RangeLowerBound<Box<Value>>, upper: RangeUpperBound<Box<Value>>. Each RangeBound has inclusive: bool, bound: Option<Box<Value>>. So we need to convert lower.bound and upper.bound from Option<Box<Value>> to Option<Datum> by calling into_datum. So:

let lower_datum = range.inner.as_ref().and_then(|i| i.lower.bound.as_ref()).map(|v| v.into_datum(buf, elem_pg_type));
That gives Option<Result<Datum, IntoDatumError>>. We need Option<Datum> with ? so we need to transpose or match. So:
let lower_datum = match range.inner.as_ref().and_then(|i| i.lower.bound.as_ref()) {
  None => None,
  Some(v) => Some(v.as_ref().into_datum(buf, elem_pg_type)?),  // but into_datum consumes self, so we need to get the value out. We have range.into_bounds(...) which consumes range. So we need to convert bounds one by one. Let me do it by destructuring.
};

Actually the issue is into_datum consumes self. So we have range: Range<Box<Value>>. We need to take inner, then for lower and upper bounds call into_datum. So:
let inner = range.inner;
let (lower_bound, upper_bound) = match inner {
  None => (None, None), // empty range
  Some(RangeInner { lower, upper }) => {
    let lower_datum = lower.bound.map(|elem| elem.into_datum(buf, elem_pg_type)).transpose()?;
    let upper_datum = upper.bound.map(|elem| elem.into_datum(buf, elem_pg_type)).transpose()?;
    (lower_datum, upper_datum)  // we need to build RangeLowerBound and RangeUpperBound with inclusive flags
  }
};
We have lower.inclusive, lower.bound -> lower_datum, upper.inclusive, upper.bound -> upper_datum. So we need RangeLowerBound { inclusive: lower.inclusive, bound: lower_datum } and same for upper. But we consumed inner and lower/upper. So:

let range = match range.inner {
  None => Range::new(None),
  Some(RangeInner { lower, upper }) => {
    let lower_datum = lower.bound.map(|elem| elem.into_datum(buf, elem_pg_type)).transpose()?;
    let upper_datum = upper.bound.map(|elem| elem.into_datum(buf, elem_pg_type)).transpose()?;
    Range::new(Some((
      RangeLowerBound { inclusive: lower.inclusive, bound: lower_datum },
      RangeUpperBound { inclusive: upper.inclusive, bound: upper_datum },
    )))
  }
};
Then buf.try_make_datum(|packer| packer.push_range(range).map_err(Into::into))?
