<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

So try_push_array takes I: IntoIterator<Item = D> where D: Borrow<Datum<'a>>. So we can pass elements.into_iter() if elements is Vec<Datum<'a>> - Datum<'a> implements Borrow<Datum<'a>> (or we need to pass references). Actually Borrow is implemented for T: Borrow<T>, so Datum borrows to itself. So elements.into_iter() yields Datum<'a> which implements Borrow<Datum<'a>>. Good.

For Map I'm building map_elems with Datum values - but push_dict_with pushes to a row. The issue is we're pushing to a RowPacker and the Datum references (e.g. from push_string) need to live. We're building map_elems as Vec of Datums that reference buf (arena). Then we do make_datum with a closure that uses map_elems - so the closure captures map_elems. The datums in map_elems reference buf, and we're also using buf.make_datum. So the arena is the same. When we push to the row in push_dict_with we're pushing Datum references - those point into the arena. So when we later push_unary_row that row's data will contain offsets into... actually the row has its own data. So when we do row.push(chunk[0]) we're pushing a Datum that might be Datum::String(&str) pointing into the arena. So the row stores that. Then push_unary_row(row) copies the row's data into the arena. So the row's data contains the string bytes or a reference - actually in repr, Row stores serialized data. So push(Datum::String(s)) serializes the string into the row. So we're good - the Datums we're pushing get serialized into the row. So the row doesn't hold references to the arena for the dict contents; it holds the serialized form. So we're good. But wait - we're building map_elems with buf.push_string(k) - so we're allocating the keys in the arena. Then we push Datum::String(...) which is a reference to that. So when we do row.push(chunk[0]) we're pushing a Datum that references the arena. The row's serialization - I need to check. Actually Row stores datums in a serialized binary form. So when we push a Datum::String(s), it gets encoded into the row buffer. So the row buffer might contain the string bytes or an offset. Let me not worry - the original code did row.push(Datum::String(&k)) and row.push(elem.into_datum(...)). So the key was a reference to the owned string k (from the map), and the value was from into_datum. So the key was &str from the map key. In our case we're doing buf.push_string(k) which allocates in the arena and returns a reference - so we're storing the key in the arena. Then we push that reference. So we have Datum::String(arena_ref). When we serialize that into the row, the row will contain the string bytes (or a reference into the arena?). Typically in such arenas the row might store offsets into the arena. So the row and the arena are used together. So push_unary_row(row) probably moves the row data into the arena. So we're good. Let me double-check the Map case - in the original we had row.push(Datum::String(&k)) - k was the owned String from the BTreeMap. So the Datum held a reference to that. Now we're doing buf.push_string(k) - k is the String, we're copying it into the arena and getting a reference back. So we have Datum::String(arena_ref). So the lifetime is tied to the arena. Good.

Let me check if there are any issues with the Map iteration - we're consuming map (the BTreeMap), and for each (k, v) we push_string(k) - so k is moved. Good. And we're building map_elems which we then iterate in chunks. But the order of iteration for a BTreeMap is deterministic. When we do push_dict_with, we're pushing key, value, key, value. So chunk[0] is key, chunk[1] is value. Good.

One more thing - we need to fix decode_text_into_row which also calls into_datum and push_range. Let me find that.

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

I need to fix decode_text_into_row's Type::Range branch to build the range fallibly (same pattern as into_datum) and use ? instead of .unwrap(). The return type of decode_text_into_row is Result<(), Box<dyn Error + Sync + Send>>, so we need to map IntoDatumError into that. We can do .map_err(|e| e.to_string().into()) or implement From<IntoDatumError> for Box<dyn Error + Sync + Send>. The simplest is to do .map_err(|e| e.to_string().into()) when we call the fallible code.
</details></think>

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

decode_text_into_row returns Result<(), Box<dyn Error + Sync + Send>>. The ? on map_err(|e| e.to_string())? - push_range returns Result<(), InvalidRangeError>. So we need to convert InvalidRangeError to Box<dyn Error + Sync + Send>. The .map_err(|e| e.to_string()) gives us Result<(), String>. And String can be converted to Box<dyn Error + Sync + Send> via .into() because we're in a function that returns Result<(), Box<dyn Error + Sync + Send>>. So we need .map_err(|e: InvalidRangeError| e.to_string().into()) for push_range. Actually the current code does .map_err(|e| e.to_string())? - the ? will try to convert the Err type to the return type. The return type is Result<(), Box<dyn Error + Sync + Send>>. So Err(String) doesn't automatically convert to Err(Box<dyn Error>). We need .map_err(|e| e.to_string().into()) so we get Box<dyn Error>. Let me check - in Rust, Box<dyn Error> implements From<String> I believe. So e.to_string().into() gives Box<dyn Error + Send + Sync> from String? Let me check - From<String> for Box<dyn Error> - I think so. So we need .map_err(|e| e.to_string().into()) for the push_range line. And for the transpose().map_err(...)? we're returning Err(String) - and the function returns Result<(), Box<dyn Error + Sync + Send>>. So we need to convert String to Box<dyn Error>. So .map_err(|e: IntoDatumError| e.to_string().into())? 
</details></think>

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

StorageErrorX - the map_err needs to produce StorageErrorX. Let me check - StorageErrorXKind::invalid_record_batch(...).with_context(...) - does that return StorageErrorX? The existing decode_text uses .map_err(|err| StorageErrorXKind::invalid_record_batch(err.to_string()).with_context("decode_text"))? so that returns something that converts to StorageErrorX via ?. So our map_err should work the same way. Let me check if there's an Into impl - the return is Result<usize, StorageErrorX>, so we need Err(StorageErrorX). The .into() at the end of the other return is Err(StorageErrorXKind::...().into()). So we need to make sure our map_err returns something that can be ? into StorageErrorX. So StorageErrorXKind::invalid_record_batch(...).with_context(...) - does with_context return StorageErrorXKind or StorageErrorX? If it returns StorageErrorXKind we need .into(). Let me check - the decode_text line doesn't have .into(), so with_context might return something that implements Into<StorageErrorX> or the ? might do the conversion. I'll leave as is since linter didn't complain.

Let me run cargo check to see if everything compiles. Actually the user can do that. Let me also verify we didn't miss the internal use in value.rs - the from_datum path. Let me check if Value::from_datum calls into_datum - no, from_datum is the opposite direction (Datum -> Value). So we're good.

Let me double-check the Map branch - we're iterating map and building map_elems. But we're consuming map, and for each (k, v) we do buf.push_string(k) - so k is moved into push_string. And we're building map_elems which has Datum::String(...) and the value datums. Then we do make_datum with push_dict_with. But push_dict_with takes a closure that pushes to a row - and we're pushing chunk[0] and chunk[1]. The issue is that map_elems holds Datums that reference the arena (from push_string and from into_datum). When we do make_datum, we're in a closure that runs packer.push_dict_with(...). So we're pushing those datums into the packer. The packer is building a new row. So we're serializing those datums into the row. The row will contain the serialized form. So when we then do push_unary_row(row) that row gets pushed to the arena. So the arena now has the row data. But the row data might contain references to other parts of the arena (the strings we pushed). So we need to make sure the order is correct - we're first building map_elems which uses buf.push_string(k) and into_datum. So the arena has the keys and the value datums might reference the arena too. Then we make_datum which creates a new row and pushes the dict. So the row's data will reference... actually in repr, when you push a Datum to a Row, does it copy the data or store a reference? For Datum::String(&str) it would typically store the bytes. So the row would contain the string bytes. So we're good. Let me leave it.

One more thing - try_make_datum. We're calling buf.try_make_datum(|packer| packer.push_range(range).map_err(Into::into)). The push_range returns Result<(), InvalidRangeError>. We need to map that to IntoDatumError for the try_make_datum's E parameter. So map_err(Into::into) converts InvalidRangeError to IntoDatumError. And try_make_datum returns Result<Datum, E>. So we get Result<Datum, IntoDatumError>. Good.
</details></think>

---


Summary of changes:

**1. `src/pgrepr/src/value.rs`**
- **Imports:** `RangeLowerBound`, `RangeUpperBound`, and `crate::value::error::IntoDatumError`.
- **`into_datum`** now returns `Result<Datum<'a>, IntoDatumError>`:
  - **Array:** Elements are collected with `into_datum` and `?`; `try_push_array` is used inside `try_make_datum` with `.map_err(Into::into)?`.
  - **List:** Same pattern: collect with `?`, then `buf.make_datum(|packer| packer.push_list(...))`.
  - **Map:** Values converted with `into_datum` and `?` into a vec, then `make_datum` + `push_dict_with` to push key/value pairs.
  - **Range:** Range is built by hand from `range.inner`: lower/upper bounds converted with `into_datum` and `.transpose()?`, then `Range::new(Some((RangeLowerBound { ... }, RangeUpperBound { ... })))`, and `buf.try_make_datum(|packer| packer.push_range(range).map_err(Into::into))?`.
  - All other variants wrapped in `Ok(...)`.
- **`decode_text_into_row` (Type::Range):** Same manual range construction and `push_range(...).map_err(|e| e.to_string().into())?` so errors become `Box<dyn Error + Sync + Send>`.

**2. Call sites**
- **pgcopy (copy.rs):** `value.into_datum(&buf, typ)` is matched; on `Err(err)` return `Err(io::Error::new(InvalidData, msg))`.
- **pgwire (protocol.rs):** Same; on `Err` return `self.error(ErrorResponse::error(INVALID_PARAMETER_VALUE, msg)).await`.
- **environmentd (http/sql.rs):** Same; on `Err` return `SqlResult::err(client, Error::Unstructured(msg)).into()`.
- **storage-operators (csv.rs):** `value.into_datum(&arena, typ).map_err(|err| StorageErrorXKind::invalid_record_batch(err.to_string()).with_context("into_datum"))?` then `packer.push(datum)`.

Lints are clean. Run `cargo check -p mz-pgrepr` and then a full `cargo check` (or build the binaries that use pgcopy, pgwire, etc.) to confirm. The COPY invalid-range pgtest should now get an `ErrorResponse` instead of a panic.

---

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

I'll add a free function or an associated function that converts Range<Box<Value>> to Result<Range<Datum<'a>>, IntoDatumError>. It needs to live in value.rs and take (range, buf, element_type). Then both call sites use it. For decode_text_into_row we'll need to map the error to Box<dyn Error + Sync + Send> with .map_err(|e| e.to_string().into())? when calling the helper.

Let me add the function - I'll put it as a private helper in the value module, right before the impl Value block or inside it. Actually the function doesn't need to be a method on Value - it's a standalone helper that takes Range<Box<Value>>. I'll add it as a free function at module level, or as a private function. Let me add it right after the imports and before the impl block. It needs to be something like:

fn value_range_to_datum_range<'a>(
    range: Range<Box<Value>>,
    buf: &'a RowArena,
    element_type: &Type,
) -> Result<Range<Datum<'a>>, IntoDatumError> {
    match range.inner {
        None => Ok(Range::new(None)),
        Some(RangeInner { lower, upper }) => {
            let lower_datum = lower
                .bound
                .map(|elem| elem.into_datum(buf, element_type))
                .transpose()?;
            let upper_datum = upper
                .bound
                .map(|elem| elem.into_datum(buf, element_type))
                .transpose()?;
            Ok(Range::new(Some((
                RangeLowerBound { inclusive: lower.inclusive, bound: lower_datum },
                RangeUpperBound { inclusive: upper.inclusive, bound: upper_datum },
            ))))
        }
    }
}

Then in into_datum we use:
let range = value_range_to_datum_range(range, buf, elem_pg_type)?;

And in decode_text_into_row we use:
let range = value_range_to_datum_range(range, &buf, element_type).map_err(|e| e.to_string().into())?;
</details></think>

Summary of changes:

**New helper** (in `value.rs`, right before `impl Value`):

- **`value_range_to_datum_range<'a>(range, buf, element_type)`** – takes a `Range<Box<Value>>`, a `&RowArena`, and the range element `Type`, and returns `Result<Range<Datum<'a>>, IntoDatumError>`. It does the `range.inner` match and builds `RangeLowerBound` / `RangeUpperBound` with `into_datum` and `?`.

**Call sites:**

- **`into_datum` (Value::Range):** Uses `let range = value_range_to_datum_range(range, buf, elem_pg_type)?;` then `buf.try_make_datum(...)` as before.
- **`decode_text_into_row` (Type::Range):** Uses `let range = value_range_to_datum_range(range, &buf, element_type).map_err(|e| e.to_string().into())?;` then `packer.push_range(range).map_err(...)?`.

Range construction now lives in one place. Lints are clean.

_**User**_

The three callers of into_datum looked very similar for error handling, can we extract this somehow?

---

_**Agent (model default, mode Agent)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

The user wants to extract the similar error-handling pattern from the three callers of into_datum. Let me look at what each caller does:

1. **pgcopy (copy.rs):** 
```rust
Ok(value) => match value.into_datum(&buf, typ) {
    Ok(datum) => row.push(datum),
    Err(err) => {
        let msg = format!("unable to decode column: {}", err);
        return Err(io::Error::new(io::ErrorKind::InvalidData, msg));
    }
},
```

2. **pgwire (protocol.rs):**
```rust
Ok(param) => match param.into_datum(&buf, &pg_typ) {
    Ok(datum) => datum,
    Err(err) => {
        let msg = format!("unable to decode parameter: {}", err);
        return self.error(ErrorResponse::error(SqlState::INVALID_PARAMETER_VALUE, msg)).await;
    }
},
```

3. **environmentd (http/sql.rs):**
```rust
Ok(param) => match param.into_datum(&buf, &pg_typ) {
    Ok(datum) => datum,
    Err(err) => {
        let msg = anyhow!("unable to decode parameter: {}", err);
        return Ok(SqlResult::err(client, Error::Unstructured(msg)).into());
    }
},
```

4. **storage-operators (csv.rs):**
```rust
let datum = value.into_datum(&arena, typ).map_err(|err| {
    StorageErrorXKind::invalid_record_batch(err.to_string())
        .with_context("into_datum")
})?;
packer.push(datum);
```

So the pattern is: call `value.into_datum(...)`, and on Err, convert to the appropriate error type for that crate. The conversion is different in each place:
- pgcopy: io::Error::new(InvalidData, format!("unable to decode column: {}", err))
- pgwire: return self.error(ErrorResponse::...(msg)).await
- environmentd: return Ok(SqlResult::err(client, Error::Unstructured(anyhow!(msg))).into())


- storage-operators: map_err to StorageErrorXKind::invalid_record_batch(err.to_string()).with_context("into_datum")

I'll trace the exact call sites to understand the full context and error handling nuances.
</details></think>

---

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

The common pattern is:
1. We have a Value and call value.into_datum(buf, typ)
2. On Ok we use the datum
3. On Err we convert IntoDatumError to the local error type

The conversion is different in each crate. We could:

**Option A: Add a method on Value that takes a context string and returns Result<Datum, E> where E: From<IntoDatumError> or we have a trait.** That doesn't work well because each caller needs a different E.

**Option B: Add a helper in pgrepr that just does the "unwrap or format the error message" - e.g. `value.into_datum_or_decode_error(buf, typ, "column")` that returns Result<Datum, String>? Then each caller does their own thing with the String. So we'd have something like:
```rust
// In pgrepr
pub fn into_datum_with_context<'a>(self, buf: &'a RowArena, typ: &Type, context: &str) -> Result<Datum<'a>, String> {
    self.into_datum(buf, typ).map_err(|e| format!("unable to decode {}: {}", context, e))
}
```
Then:
- pgcopy: value.into_datum_with_context(&buf, typ, "column")? and they need to convert String to io::Error
- pgwire: same, then convert to protocol error
- etc.

Actually that still leaves each caller doing one line differently (converting String to their error). So we'd have:
- pgcopy: row.push(value.into_datum_with_context(&buf, typ, "column").map_err(|msg| io::Error::new(InvalidData, msg))?);
- pgwire: value.into_datum_with_context(&buf, &pg_typ, "parameter").map_err(|msg| ...)? 
So the helper could return Result<Datum, String> and each caller does .map_err(|msg| their_error(msg))? - that's one line per caller and the "format the message" is centralized.

**Option C: Extension trait that adds a method like `into_datum_unwrap_or(self, buf, typ, f: impl FnOnce(IntoDatumError) -> E)` returning Result<Datum, E>. So each caller does value.into_datum_or_else(&buf, typ, |e| io::Error::new(..., e.to_string())). So the caller supplies the conversion. That way the only shared part is the match/result handling, and the error conversion is a closure. So:

```rust
// In pgrepr
pub trait ValueIntoDatumExt {
    fn into_datum_or_else<'a, E, F>(self, buf: &'a RowArena, typ: &Type, f: F) -> Result<Datum<'a>, E>
    where
        F: FnOnce(IntoDatumError) -> E;
}

impl ValueIntoDatumExt for Value {
    fn into_datum_or_else<'a, E, F>(self, buf: &'a RowArena, typ: &Type, f: F) -> Result<Datum<'a>, E>
    where
        F: FnOnce(IntoDatumError) -> E,
    {
        self.into_datum(buf, typ).map_err(f)
    }
}
```

Then callers do:
- pgcopy: value.into_datum_or_else(&buf, typ, |e| io::Error::new(io::ErrorKind::InvalidData, format!("unable to decode column: {}", e)))? and then row.push(datum) - wait we need the datum. So it's let datum = value.into_datum_or_else(&buf, typ, |e| ...)?; row.push(datum); So the trait just wraps into_datum and lets you map the error. That's a thin wrapper.

Actually the similarity is: (1) call into_datum, (2) on Err, build a message and convert to local error type. So the only thing we can extract is either (a) a helper that returns Result<Datum, String> with a formatted message, or (b) a helper that takes a closure to map the error. Option (b) is more flexible. Let me do option (b) as an extension trait in pgrepr so all callers can do:

```rust
let datum = value.into_datum_or_else(&buf, typ, |e| make_my_error(e))?;
```

And the "make_my_error" part is still per-caller but at least the pattern is one line. Actually that's just .map_err()! So value.into_datum(&buf, typ).map_err(|e| io::Error::new(..., format!("unable to decode column: {}", e)))? - the only repetition is the format string "unable to decode column/parameter: {}". So we could add a helper that returns Result<Datum, String> with the message already formatted:

```rust
pub fn into_datum_for_decode_context<'a>(self, buf: &'a RowArena, typ: &Type, context: &str) -> Result<Datum<'a>, String> {
    self.into_datum(buf, typ).map_err(|e| format!("unable to decode {}: {}", context, e))
}
```

Then:
- pgcopy: row.push(value.into_datum_for_decode_context(&buf, typ, "column").map_err(|msg| io::Error::new(io::ErrorKind::InvalidData, msg))?);
- pgwire: value.into_datum_for_decode_context(&buf, &pg_typ, "parameter").map_err(|msg| ...)? - but pgwire doesn't return a Result<Datum, E>, it returns early with self.error(...).await. So they can't use ? with a map_err that converts to their error type. So they'd still do match value.into_datum_for_decode_context(...) { Ok(d) => d, Err(msg) => return self.error(...).await }.

So the pgwire and environmentd call sites don't use Result and ? for the outer flow - they do an early return. So a helper that returns Result<Datum, String> would let them do:
let datum = value.into_datum_for_decode_context(&buf, typ, "parameter").map_err(|msg| (msg,))?; 
No, they need to return different types. So the cleanest is:

**Provide a method that returns Result<Datum, String> with a formatted message**, e.g. 
`into_datum_decode_error(self, buf, typ, context: &str) -> Result<Datum, String>` 
that does `self.into_datum(buf, typ).map_err(|e| format!("unable to decode {}: {}", context, e))`.

Then:
- **pgcopy:** `row.push(value.into_datum_decode_error(&buf, typ, "column").map_err(io::Error::new(io::ErrorKind::InvalidData, ...))?` - we need a function that takes String and returns io::Error. So `|msg| io::Error::new(io::ErrorKind::InvalidData, msg)`.
- **pgwire:** They do `return self.error(ErrorResponse::error(..., msg)).await` - so they need to match and return. So they could do `let datum = value.into_datum_decode_error(...).map_err(|msg| return self.error(...).await)?` - no, map_err can't return from the outer function. So they'd still need a match. Unless we have the helper return Result<Datum, IntoDatumError> and they do .map_err(|e| format!("unable to decode parameter: {}", e)) and then match - that's the same as now. So the only extraction that works for all is to put the message format in one place. So:

**Helper:** `fn into_datum_with_decode_message(self, buf, typ, context: &str) -> Result<Datum, String>` 
implemented as `self.into_datum(buf, typ).map_err(|e| format!("unable to decode {}: {}", context, e))`.

Then:
- pgcopy: `Ok(value) => row.push(value.into_datum_with_decode_message(&buf, typ, "column").map_err(|msg| io::Error::new(io::ErrorKind::InvalidData, msg))?),`
- pgwire: `Ok(param) => value.into_datum_with_decode_message(&buf, &pg_typ, "parameter").map_err(|msg| return self.error(ErrorResponse::error(..., msg)).await)?` - that doesn't work, you can't return from inside map_err. So pgwire has to do a match. So we'd have:
  `Ok(param) => match value.into_datum_with_decode_message(&buf, &pg_typ, "parameter") { Ok(d) => d, Err(msg) => return self.error(...).await }`
  So we've shortened the Err arm from formatting the message to just using msg.
- environmentd: same.
- storage-operators: they use a different message ("into_datum" as context). So they could use the helper with context "into_datum" and then .map_err(|msg| StorageErrorXKind::invalid_record_batch(msg).with_context("into_datum"))? - wait that would duplicate "into_datum". So they could use context "record field" or just "into_datum" and then map_err to add context. Actually they have .with_context("into_datum") - so the context in the message could be "record field" and the with_context stays. So value.into_datum_with_decode_message(&arena, typ, "record field").map_err(|msg| StorageErrorXKind::invalid_record_batch(msg).with_context("into_datum"))?.

So the helper is: add to Value (or as a free function in pgrepr):
```rust
pub fn into_datum_with_decode_message<'a>(self, buf: &'a RowArena, typ: &Type, context: &str) -> Result<Datum<'a>, String> {
    self.into_datum(buf, typ).map_err(|e| format!("unable to decode {}: {}", context, e))
}
```

I'll add this to the Value impl in pgrepr and update the three (or four) call sites to use it. That way the "unable to decode X: {}" format is in one place and call sites just supply the context and how to convert String to their error.
</details></think>

---


Adding a helper on `Value` that performs the decode and formats the error message so call sites only supply the context and their error conversion.

---


Summary of changes:

**1. New helper on `Value` in `src/pgrepr/src/value.rs`**

- **`into_datum_decode_error(self, buf, typ, context: &str) -> Result<Datum<'a>, String>`**  
  Calls `into_datum` and on error maps it to  
  `"unable to decode {context}: {error}"`.  
  So the message shape lives in one place and callers only pass a short `context` and turn the `String` into their error type.

**2. Call sites**

- **pgcopy:**  
  `value.into_datum_decode_error(&buf, typ, "column").map_err(|msg| io::Error::new(io::ErrorKind::InvalidData, msg))?` then `row.push(datum)`.

- **pgwire:**  
  `match param.into_datum_decode_error(&buf, &pg_typ, "parameter") { Ok(datum) => datum, Err(msg) => return self.error(ErrorResponse::error(..., msg)).await }`.

- **environmentd:**  
  Same pattern, with `SqlResult::err(client, Error::Unstructured(anyhow!(msg)))`.

- **storage-operators:**  
  `value.into_datum_decode_error(&arena, typ, "record field").map_err(|msg| StorageErrorXKind::invalid_record_batch(msg).with_context("into_datum"))?`.

So every caller uses the same `"unable to decode {context}: {error}"` message and only supplies `context` and the conversion from `String` to their error type. Lints are clean.

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

So the function returns `Result<StatementResult, Error>`. So when we do `return Ok(SqlResult::err(...).into())`, we're returning `Ok` of a **successful** Result - meaning "we're not panicking, we handled the error." The inner value is `StatementResult`, which is the thing that gets sent to the client. So:
- `SqlResult::err(client, Error::Unstructured(anyhow!(msg)))` builds an `SqlResult::Err { error, notices }` that represents "this statement failed with this error."
- `.into()` turns that `SqlResult` into a `StatementResult` (via `StatementResult::SqlResult(res)`).
- We `return Ok(...)` so the async function returns `Ok(StatementResult)` — i.e. "we're returning a statement result (which happens to be an error result) successfully."

So the somewhat confusing part is: we're returning **Ok** of an **error result**. The outer Result is "did we complete the HTTP/execution flow without crashing?" The inner StatementResult/SqlResult carries "what to send to the client," which in this case is an error response. So the line means: "Stop here and send the client an error response (unable to decode parameter: {msg}), and do it in a way that fits our return type."
</details></think>

---


That line does two things:

**1. Build an error result for the client**

- **`SqlResult::err(client, Error::Unstructured(anyhow!(msg)))`**  
  Builds a `SqlResult::Err` that will be sent to the client. It uses the HTTP SQL `Error` type and wraps the decode failure message in `anyhow!(msg)` so it becomes an `Error::Unstructured` (generic user-facing error). `SqlResult::err` also attaches any session notices from `client`.

**2. Return it as the function’s success value**

- **`.into()`**  
  Converts that `SqlResult` into a `StatementResult` (via `From`: `StatementResult::SqlResult(sql_result)`).

- **`return Ok( ... )`**  
  The function’s signature is `-> Result<StatementResult, Error>`. So we return **`Ok(statement_result)`**: the execution path “succeeded” (no bug, no unexpected error), and the value we’re returning is a **statement result that describes an error** (decode failure). The caller will then serialize that `StatementResult` and send it to the client (e.g. as an error in the SQL-over-HTTP response).

So in plain terms: **“Return a value that tells the caller to send the client an error response for ‘unable to decode parameter: {msg}’, and do it by returning Ok(...) so we satisfy the function’s return type.”** The “error” is the *content* of the result we’re returning, not the outer `Result` (which is `Ok`).

The compiler can't infer the type for `Into::into` and for the `.into()` in the decode_text_into_row. I need to use explicit type annotations:

1. Line 279: `.map_err(Into::into)` - the closure is used in try_make_datum which returns Result<Datum, E> where E is the error from the closure. So we need the error to be IntoDatumError. So we can use `.map_err(IntoDatumError::from)` or be explicit: `.map_err(|e| IntoDatumError::from(e))` or `.map_err(Into::<IntoDatumError>::into)`.

2. Line 362: same - `packer.push_range(range).map_err(Into::into)` - push_range returns Result<(), InvalidRangeError>, and we need IntoDatumError. So `.map_err(IntoDatumError::from)` or `.map_err(Into::<IntoDatumError>::into)`.

3. Line 851: `.map_err(|e| e.to_string().into())?` - we're in decode_text_into_row which returns Result<(), Box<dyn Error + Sync + Send>>. So we need to convert the error to Box<dyn Error + Sync + Send>. So we could do `.map_err(|e: InvalidRangeError| e.to_string().into())` but the issue is that String implements Into for several things. We need to be explicit: `.map_err(|e| -> Box<dyn Error + Sync + Send> { e.to_string().into() })` or use Box::from(e.to_string()) or similar. Actually in Rust, Box<dyn Error> doesn't implement From<String> in std - let me check. Actually the error says "cannot satisfy _: From<std::string::String>" - so the compiler doesn't know what type we're converting to. So we need to annotate so it knows we want Box<dyn Error + Sync + Send>. We can do: `.map_err(|e| Box::<dyn Error + Sync + Send>::from(e.to_string()))` - does Box implement From<String>? Yes, Box<dyn Error> from the error trait - actually Box<dyn Error> might be from the crate that defines Error. Let me try `.map_err(|e| e.to_string() as Box<dyn Error + Sync + Send>)` - no. The usual pattern is `Err(e.into())` or `.map_err(Into::into)`. So for String to Box<dyn Error + Sync + Send>, we need a conversion. In Rust standard library, I don't think String implements Into<Box<dyn Error>>. So we might need to use a different approach. Let me check - actually in many codebases they do `format!("...").into()` and the return type of the function is Result<(), Box<dyn Error>> so the compiler infers. So the issue might be that the ? is there and the type flows from the return type - but we have multiple ? in that match arm and the error type might not be flowing. Let me just use an explicit type: `.map_err(|e: InvalidRangeError| Box::<dyn std::error::Error + Send + Sync>::from(e.to_string()))?` - Box::from(str) - does Box implement From<String>? For Box<dyn Error>, I think we need to use a different approach. Actually the original code before my changes was `.map_err(|e| e.to_string().into())?` and it was in a function returning Result<(), Box<dyn Error + Sync + Send>>. So the ? would propagate an Err. The Err type would need to be compatible with Box<dyn Error + Sync + Send>. So we need something that converts to Box<dyn Error + Sync + Send>. String - in std, String doesn't implement Into<Box<dyn Error>>. So maybe the codebase has an impl or they use a different pattern. Let me look at the full context of line 851.

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

So they use `"something".into()` for Err - that's &str or String being converted. In Rust std, Box<dyn Error> doesn't have From<String>. But the anyhow crate and other crates might add it. Let me check - the function signature is Result<(), Box<dyn Error + Sync + Send>>. So when we do .map_err(|e| e.to_string().into()) the compiler needs to find what type T is such that Result<(), T> when used with ? in a function returning Result<(), Box<dyn Error + Sync + Send>> - so T must be convertible to Box<dyn Error + Sync + Send>. So we need String to implement Into<Box<dyn Error + Sync + Send>>. In standard library, I don't think so. So maybe the fix is to use a different conversion. Let me try: .map_err(|e| Box::<dyn Error + Sync + Send>::from(e.to_string())) - Box::from for String might give Box<str>. So we need another approach. One option is to use IntoDatumError and then convert: .map_err(|e: IntoDatumError| Box::<dyn Error + Sync + Send>::from(e)) - and IntoDatumError implements Error, so Box::from(impl Error) might work! So Box::<dyn Error + Sync + Send>::from(err) works when err implements Error + Send + Sync. So for value_range_to_datum_range we get Result<Range, IntoDatumError>. So we could .map_err(|e| Box::<dyn Error + Sync + Send>::from(e)) for the IntoDatumError. And for push_range we get InvalidRangeError, so we need .map_err(|e| Box::<dyn Error + Sync + Send>::from(e)) - and InvalidRangeError implements Error. So we could do:
- value_range_to_datum_range(...).map_err(|e| Box::<dyn Error + Sync + Send>::from(e))? 
- packer.push_range(range).map_err(|e| Box::<dyn Error + Sync + Send>::from(e))?

That way we're converting the error types to Box<dyn Error + Sync + Send> explicitly. Let me check if InvalidRangeError implements Send + Sync - it likely does. And IntoDatumError too.
</details></think>

---


Summary of changes:

1. **Lines 279 and 362**  
   Replaced `.map_err(Into::into)` with `.map_err(IntoDatumError::from)` so the error type is clearly `IntoDatumError` (from `InvalidArrayError` and `InvalidRangeError`).

2. **Lines 851–852**  
   Replaced `.map_err(|e| e.to_string().into())?` with `.map_err(|e| Box::<dyn Error + Sync + Send>::from(e))?`. The target type is now explicit, and we convert the error (e.g. `IntoDatumError` / `InvalidRangeError`) to `Box<dyn Error + Sync + Send>` via `Error` instead of going through `String`, which the compiler couldn’t tie to that boxed type.

Lints are clean. Run `cargo check -p mz-pgrepr` to confirm it compiles.

```
[33mcommit 26b315e614c0c80c04b56d1414323f7522222b0b[m
Author: mtabebe &lt;michael.abebe@materialize.com&gt;
Date:   Wed Feb 18 13:41:21 2026 -0500

    [10089] [Adapter] Introduce Result/Error in into_datum and be graceful
    
    Problem:
    into_datum has no way to report an error, so if we hand it a malformed
    range (e.g., with copy that assumes datums are correctly formed) then it
    will panic.
    
    Solution:
    Make into_datum return a Result and also an Error. Propagate this error
    in the callers in a few places where appropriate.
    
    Testing:
    New pgwire test (copy-from-range.pt) that reproduced the issue
    
    Manual:
    &#96;&#96;&#96;
    materialize=&gt; create table t(r int4range);
    CREATE TABLE
    materialize=&gt; INSERT INTO t VALUES (int4range(7, 10));
    INSERT 0 1
    materialize=&gt; INSERT INTO t VALUES (int4range(7, 3));
    ERROR:  range lower bound must be less than or equal to range upper bound
    materialize=&gt; COPY t FROM STDIN;
    Enter data to be copied followed by a newline.
    End with a backslash and a period on a line by itself, or an EOF signal.
    &gt;&gt; (3, 5)
    &gt;&gt; COPY 1
    materialize=&gt; COPY t FROM STDIN;
    Enter data to be copied followed by a newline.
    End with a backslash and a period on a line by itself, or an EOF signal.
    &gt;&gt; (5, 3)
    &gt;&gt; ERROR:  unable to decode column: range lower bound must be less than or equal to range upper bound
    &#96;&#96;&#96;

 src/environmentd/tests/pgwire.rs                |   5 [32m+[m
 src/pgcopy/src/copy.rs                          |   8 [32m+[m[31m-[m

```
```
[10089] [Adapter] Introduce Result/Error in into_datum and be graceful

Problem:
into_datum has no way to report an error, so if we hand it a malformed
range (e.g., with copy that assumes datums are correctly formed) then it
will panic.

Solution:
Make into_datum return a Result and also an Error. Propagate this error
in the callers in a few places where appropriate.

Testing:
New pgwire test (copy-from-range.pt) that reproduced the issue

Manual:
&#96;&#96;&#96;
materialize=&gt; create table t(r int4range);
CREATE TABLE
materialize=&gt; INSERT INTO t VALUES (int4range(7, 10));
INSERT 0 1
materialize=&gt; INSERT INTO t VALUES (int4range(7, 3));
ERROR:  range lower bound must be less than or equal to range upper bound
materialize=&gt; COPY t FROM STDIN;
Enter data to be copied followed by a newline.
End with a backslash and a period on a line by itself, or an EOF signal.
&gt;&gt; (3, 5)
&gt;&gt; COPY 1
materialize=&gt; COPY t FROM STDIN;
Enter data to be copied followed by a newline.
End with a backslash and a period on a line by itself, or an EOF signal.
&gt;&gt; (5, 3)
&gt;&gt; ERROR:  unable to decode column: range lower bound must be less than or equal to range upper bound
&#96;&#96;&#96;


```

</tool-use>

---


Summary of what changed:

**Title**
- **Before:** "Introduce Result/Error in into_datum and be graceful"
- **After:** "Fix COPY panic on invalid range by making into_datum return Result"  
  (States the bug and the fix.)

**Problem**
- Shortened to one sentence.
- Clarified “malformed range” with an example and that the failure mode was a panic.

**Solution**
- Turned into a short bullet list: new error type, `into_datum` return type, `into_datum_decode_error`, where errors are propagated, and the `value_range_to_datum_range` helper.
- Dropped “in a few places where appropriate” and named the call sites.

**Testing**
- Specified that the new test is a pgtest and that it covers both valid and invalid COPY (positive + negative).

**Manual**
- Left the session output as-is so it stays reproducible.

The commit has been amended with this message (new hash `c827cbc525`). The temporary file used for the message was removed so it won’t show up in the tree.

<tool-use data-tool-type="shell" data-tool-name="run_terminal_command_v2">
<details><summary>Tool use: **run_terminal_command_v2** • Run command: cd /Users/michael.abebe/Code/materialize && git show ae17e159d1 --stat</summary>

```bash
cd /Users/michael.abebe/Code/materialize && git show ae17e159d1 --stat
```

```
[33mcommit ae17e159d1311a436d652360f5e01a8015c5b66e[m
Author: mtabebe &lt;michael.abebe@materialize.com&gt;
Date:   Wed Feb 18 13:41:21 2026 -0500

    [10089] [Adapter] Fix COPY panic on invalid range by making into_datum return Result
    
    Problem:
    into_datum had no way to report errors, so COPY with a malformed range
    (e.g. [7,3) with lower &gt; upper) caused a panic instead of a user-visible error.
    
    Solution:
    - Introduce IntoDatumError (wrapping InvalidRangeError and InvalidArrayError)
    and make Value::into_datum return Result&lt;Datum, IntoDatumError&gt;.
    - Add into_datum_decode_error for decode/parameter contexts with a consistent
    error message
    - Propagate the error at all call sites (pgcopy, pgwire bind, environmentd
    HTTP SQL params, storage-operators CSV).
    - Extract value_range_to_datum_range for shared range conversion.
    
    Testing:
    - New pgtest copy-from-range.pt: positive COPY cases (valid ranges) and
      negative case (invalid range [7,3) expects ErrorResponse).
    
    Manual:
    &#96;&#96;&#96;
    materialize=&gt; create table t(r int4range);
    CREATE TABLE
    materialize=&gt; INSERT INTO t VALUES (int4range(7, 10));
    INSERT 0 1
    materialize=&gt; INSERT INTO t VALUES (int4range(7, 3));
    ERROR:  range lower bound must be less than or equal to range upper bound
    materialize=&gt; COPY t FROM STDIN;
    Enter data to be copied followed by a newline.
    End with a backslash and a period on a line by itself, or an EOF signal.
    &gt;&gt; (3, 5)
    &gt;&gt; COPY 1
    materialize=&gt; COPY t FROM STDIN;
    Enter data to be copied followed by a newline.
    End with a backslash and a period on a line by itself, or an EOF signal.
    &gt;&gt; (5, 3)
    &gt;&gt; ERROR:  unable to decode column: range lower bound must be less than or equal to range upper bound
    &#96;&#96;&#96;

 src/environmentd/src/http/sql.rs                |   9 [32m+[m[31m-[m
 src/environmentd/tests/pgwire.rs                |   5 [32m+[m
 src/pgcopy/src/copy.rs                          |   8 [32m+[m[31m-[m
 src/pgrepr/src/lib.rs                           |   1 [32m+[m
 src/pgrepr/src/value.rs                         | 123 [32m++++++++++++++++++[m[31m------[m
 src/pgrepr/src/value/error.rs                   |  55 [32m+++++++++++[m
 src/pgwire/src/protocol.rs                      |  12 [32m++[m[31m-[m
 src/storage-operators/src/oneshot_source/csv.rs |   7 [32m+[m[31m-[m
 test/pgtest/copy-from-range.pt                  | 110 [32m+++++++++++++++++++++[m
 9 files changed, 295 insertions(+), 35 deletions(-)

```
</details>
</tool-use>

```
[35m@@ -0,0 +1,55 @@[m
[32m+[m[32m// Copyright Materialize, Inc. and contributors. All rights reserved.[m

[32m+[m[32m//! Error type for fallible conversion from [&#96;super::Value&#96;] to [&#96;mz_repr::Datum&#96;].[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32muse std::error::Error;[m
[32m+[m[32muse std::fmt;[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32muse mz_repr::adt::array::InvalidArrayError;[m
[32m+[m[32muse mz_repr::adt::range::InvalidRangeError;[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32m/// Errors that can occur when converting a [&#96;super::Value&#96;] into a [&#96;mz_repr::Datum&#96;].[m
[32m+[m[32m#[derive(Debug)][m
[32m+[m[32mpub enum IntoDatumError {[m
[32m+[m[32m    /// Invalid range (e.g. misordered bounds).[m
[32m+[m[32m    Range(InvalidRangeError),[m
[32m+[m[32m    /// Invalid array (e.g. wrong cardinality or too many dimensions).[m
[32m+[m[32m    Array(InvalidArrayError),[m
[32m+[m[32m}[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32mimpl fmt::Display for IntoDatumError {[m
[32m+[m[32m    fn fmt(&amp;self, f: &amp;mut fmt::Formatter&lt;'_&gt;) -&gt; fmt::Result {[m
[32m+[m[32m        match self {[m
[32m+[m[32m            IntoDatumError::Range(e) =&gt; e.fmt(f),[m
[32m+[m[32m            IntoDatumError::Array(e) =&gt; e.fmt(f),[m
[32m+[m[32m        }[m
[32m+[m[32m    }[m
[32m+[m[32m}[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32mimpl Error for IntoDatumError {[m
[32m+[m[32m    fn source(&amp;self) -&gt; Option&lt;&amp;(dyn Error + 'static)&gt; {[m
[32m+[m[32m        match self {[m
[32m+[m[32m            IntoDatumError::Range(e) =&gt; Some(e),[m
[32m+[m[32m            IntoDatumError::Array(e) =&gt; Some(e),[m
[32m+[m[32m        }[m
[32m+[m[32m    }[m
[32m+[m[32m}[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32mimpl From&lt;InvalidRangeError&gt; for IntoDatumError {[m
[32m+[m[32m    fn from(e: InvalidRangeError) -&gt; Self {[m
[32m+[m[32m        IntoDatumError::Range(e)[m
[32m+[m[32m    }[m
[32m+[m[32m}[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32mimpl From&lt;InvalidArrayError&gt; for IntoDatumError {[m
[32m+[m[32m    fn from(e: InvalidArrayError) -&gt; Self {[m
[32m+[m[32m        IntoDatumError::Array(e)[m
[32m+[m[32m    }[m
[32m+[m[32m}[m

[32m+[m[32m                                ))[m
[32m+[m[32m                                .await;[m

[32m+[m[32m                    },[m
                     Err(err) =&gt; {[m
                         let msg = format!("unable to decode parameter: {}", err);[m
                         return self[m

[32m+[m[32m                packer.push(datum);[m

         }[m

[35m@@ -0,0 +1,110 @@[m
[32m+[m[32m# COPY with int4range: positive cases (valid ranges) and one negative (invalid range).[m
[32m+[m[32m# Invalid case: lower &gt; upper should return an error, not panic.[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32msend[m
[32m+[m[32mQuery {"query": "DROP TABLE IF EXISTS copy_range_t"}[m
[32m+[m[32m----[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32muntil ignore=NoticeResponse[m
[32m+[m[32mReadyForQuery[m
[32m+[m[32m----[m
[32m+[m[32mCommandComplete {"tag":"DROP TABLE"}[m
[32m+[m[32mReadyForQuery {"status":"I"}[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32msend[m
[32m+[m[32mQuery {"query": "CREATE TABLE copy_range_t (r int4range)"}[m
[32m+[m[32m----[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32muntil[m
[32m+[m[32mReadyForQuery[m
[32m+[m[32m----[m
[32m+[m[32mCommandComplete {"tag":"CREATE TABLE"}[m
[32m+[m[32mReadyForQuery {"status":"I"}[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32m#[m
[32m+[m[32m# Positive: valid ranges[m
[32m+[m[32m#[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32m# single valid row [1,10)[m
[32m+[m[32msend[m
[32m+[m[32mQuery {"query": "COPY copy_range_t FROM STDIN"}[m
[32m+[m[32m----[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32muntil[m
[32m+[m[32mCopyIn[m
[32m+[m[32m----[m
[32m+[m[32mCopyIn {"format":"text","column_formats":["text"]}[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32msend[m
[32m+[m[32mCopyData "[1,10)\n"[m
[32m+[m[32mCopyDone[m
[32m+[m[32m----[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32muntil[m
[32m+[m[32mReadyForQuery[m
[32m+[m[32m----[m
[32m+[m[32mCommandComplete {"tag":"COPY 1"}[m
[32m+[m[32mReadyForQuery {"status":"I"}[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32m# multiple valid rows[m
[32m+[m[32msend[m
[32m+[m[32mQuery {"query": "COPY copy_range_t FROM STDIN"}[m
[32m+[m[32m----[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32muntil[m
[32m+[m[32mCopyIn[m
[32m+[m[32m----[m
[32m+[m[32mCopyIn {"format":"text","column_formats":["text"]}[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32msend[m
[32m+[m[32mCopyData "[0,0)\n"[m
[32m+[m[32mCopyData "empty\n"[m
[32m+[m[32mCopyData "(,)\n"[m
[32m+[m[32mCopyDone[m
[32m+[m[32m----[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32muntil[m
[32m+[m[32mReadyForQuery[m
[32m+[m[32m----[m
[32m+[m[32mCommandComplete {"tag":"COPY 3"}[m
[32m+[m[32mReadyForQuery {"status":"I"}[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32m# verify contents[m
[32m+[m[32msend[m
[32m+[m[32mQuery {"query": "SELECT r FROM copy_range_t ORDER BY r"}[m
[32m+[m[32m----[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32muntil[m
[32m+[m[32mReadyForQuery[m
[32m+[m[32m----[m

[32m+[m[32mDataRow {"fields":["empty"]}[m
[32m+[m[32mDataRow {"fields":["empty"]}[m
[32m+[m[32mDataRow {"fields":["(,)"]}[m
[32m+[m[32mDataRow {"fields":["[1,10)"]}[m
[32m+[m[32mCommandComplete {"tag":"SELECT 4"}[m
[32m+[m[32mReadyForQuery {"status":"I"}[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32m#[m

[32m+[m[32m#[m
```

```json
                  "content": "Materialize is unable to communicate to users why it takes a long time"

              "file": "doc/developer/design/20231027_refresh_mvs.md",
              "matches": [
                {
                  "lineNumber": 43,
                  "content": "Note that other systems also expose similar knobs. Making this trade-off tunable by users is an important point also in the [Napa paper](http://www.vldb.org/pvldb/vol14/p2986-sankaranarayanan.pdf). Snowflake's dynamic tables allow the user to configure a [\"target lag\"](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/dynamic-tables-refresh#understanding-target-lag)."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 65,
                  "content": "We are not aiming to provide hard guarantees that we'll refresh a materialized view at the specified time. This is because the solution approaches considered here are based on spinning up replicas near the refresh times, which is not always possible due to AWS capacity issues. Therefore, timely completion of refreshes are only on a best-effort basis, with possibly tunable knobs by power users that control the trade-off of cost vs. chances that refreshes will complete on time."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 240,
                  "content": "As mentioned before, simply keeping the cluster of an MV at 0 replicas most of the time and creating a replica when a refresh should happen would satisfy success criteria 1. and 2., but not 3. and 4. We initially suggested this as a workaround [to a big user](https://materializeinc.slack.com/archives/C053EPHMU05/p1698173967255099), but it turned out that it's not suitable for them, because they would like to consume a unified view of the cold and hot results through PowerBI, which seems unable to perform this unioning. Therefore, they'd need to &#96;UNION&#96; the cold and hot results inside Materialize, for which they need success criterion 3. Also, not satisfying success criterion 4., i.e. holding back Persist compaction of inputs to the time of the previous refresh, would be \"low-to-medium scary\"."
                }
              ]
            },
            {
              "file": "doc/developer/design/20230903_avro_doc.md",
              "matches": [

                  "lineNumber": 356,

                }
              ]
            },
            {
              "file": "doc/developer/design/20230315_backup_restore.md",
              "matches": [
                {
                  "lineNumber": 174,
                  "content": "Materialize’s sources and sinks also store data in the systems they connect to. In general, we’re unable to back up and restore this data fully. (If we restore to a week-old backup, the data our Kafka source had ingested since then might be compacted away.)"
                }
              ]
            },
            {
              "file": "doc/developer/design/20230216_role_based_access_control.md",

                  "lineNumber": 430,

                },

                  "lineNumber": 569,

                }

              "file": "doc/developer/design/20230210_storage_persist_sink_redux.md",
              "matches": [

                  "lineNumber": 162,
                  "content": "(the technical details here are mentioned in [Conclusion and alternatives](#conclusion-and-alternatives)), which we are unable"

              "matches": [
                {
                  "lineNumber": 60,
                  "content": "However, if done naively the way queries work in materialize requires a fixed timestamp at which time the results of the queries are reported. But if one replica is lagging or otherwise unable to provide logging data, the query would either report out of date data (when settling for the least timestamp where all replicas have provided data) or block for a long time (when choosing a timestamp closer to the actual time). See the design question “Expose an aggregate sources or source per replica” as well as the [discussion in the Github](https://github.com/MaterializeInc/materialize/pull/13340#issuecomment-1180628374) PR for more technical details."
```

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

The style guide (doc/developer/style.md) says "Tricky words to avoid: unable, bad, illegal, unknown." So "unable to decode" is something to flag. However, the existing codebase already uses "unable to decode" in many places (pgcopy, pgwire, environmentd, etc.) for the same kind of error. So this change is consistent with existing usage in this code path. I could still mention it as a nit for future consistency with the style guide if they ever reword those messages.

Let me compile the full review.

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

This change is a bugfix: COPY used to panic on invalid range input; now it returns an error. That's a user-visible behavior change (error instead of crash). So a release note would be appropriate - "COPY FROM STDIN now returns an error instead of panicking when given an invalid range (e.g. lower bound greater than upper bound)." I'll mention it in the review.

I couldn't run cargo fmt or clippy (no rustup in env). I'll say "Please run locally: cargo fmt --check, cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets, bin/lint" in the review.
</details></think>

---


Here’s the review:

---

# PR review: ae17e159d1 — Fix COPY panic on invalid range (into_datum returns Result)

## Pre-merge checklist

| Item | Status |
|------|--------|
| **One semantic change per RU** | Yes. Single fix: COPY (and related paths) no longer panic on invalid range; they return a structured error. |
| **Adequate testing** | Yes. New pgtest `copy-from-range.pt`: positive COPY cases (valid ranges), SELECT to verify, and negative case (invalid `[7,3)` → ErrorResponse). Test wired in `environmentd/tests/pgwire.rs`. |
| **Release notes** | **Should add.** User-visible: COPY FROM STDIN (and bind/params) now return an error instead of crashing on invalid range. Suggest: “COPY FROM STDIN and parameter binding now return an error when given an invalid range (e.g. lower bound greater than upper bound) instead of crashing.” |
| **Documentation** | OK. `IntoDatumError` and `into_datum_decode_error` have doc comments; `value_range_to_datum_range` has a one-line comment. No user docs needed for this bugfix. |

---

## Change description and polish

- **Description:** First line is clear and imperative. Problem/solution/testing/manual sections are good.
- **Polish:** No `// XXX`, commented-out code, or stray `println!`/debug code in the diff. No unrelated churn.
- **Size:** ~295 insertions, 35 deletions across 9 files — fine for review.

---

## What to look for: tests

- **Coverage:** The new pgtest covers the failing path (invalid range over COPY) and valid COPY + SELECT. Matches guide-testing.md (observable behavior, pgtest for wire behavior).
- **Style:** Uses existing pgtest patterns; test is focused and readable.

**Nit:** The top-of-file comment in `copy-from-range.pt` that mentioned “Run: … METADATA_BACKEND_URL” appears to have been removed in this commit; if you still want that hint for local runs, it could live in `doc/developer/guide-testing.md` or a short README near the pgtest dir instead.

---

## What to look for: code style

- **Rust:** I couldn’t run `cargo fmt` or `cargo clippy` in this environment (no rustup). **Please run locally:**  
  `cargo fmt --check`  
  `cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets`  
  `bin/lint`  
  and fix any reported issues.

- **Error messages:** `doc/developer/style.md` says to avoid “unable” in error text. This change introduces “unable to decode {context}: {error}” in `into_datum_decode_error`. Existing call sites (pgcopy, pgwire, HTTP SQL) already use “unable to decode column/parameter” in the same flow, so this is consistent with current code. **Nit:** If you ever reword these errors, consider something like “decode error for {context}: {error}” to align with the style guide; not blocking.

- **New public API:** `IntoDatumError` and `into_datum_decode_error` are documented; `IntoDatumError` is re-exported from the crate. Good.

---

## What to look for: architecture and design

- **Simplicity:** Single error type, one helper for range conversion (`value_range_to_datum_range`), one helper for decode-style errors (`into_datum_decode_error`). Call sites stay small.
- **Encapsulation:** Error and conversion logic live in pgrepr; call sites only map to their own error type. No new dependencies.
- **Special casing:** No extra booleans or type-specific branches beyond what’s needed for Array/Range (and existing List/Map).

---

## Summary for the author

**Blocking**

- None.

**Strong suggestions**

1. **Release note:** Add a short release note for the user-visible change (COPY/parameter binding error instead of panic on invalid range), as in the checklist above.

**Nits**

1. Run and fix any issues from `cargo fmt --check`, `cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets`, and `bin/lint`.
2. Error message wording: “unable to decode” is discouraged in the style guide; fine to leave as-is for consistency with existing messages, but consider a wording like “decode error for {context}: {error}” when touching this area again.
3. If the “how to run this pgtest” note was intentionally removed from `copy-from-range.pt`, consider documenting it in the developer guide or a small README so future contributors can run it easily.

**Verdict:** The change fixes the COPY panic, adds a clear error path and tests, and fits the codebase. Once the release note is in place and local fmt/clippy/lint pass, this looks good to merge.

---

   --> src/pgrepr/src/value.rs:368:16

    |                ^^^^^^^^^^ no item named `into_datum` in scope

 Documenting mz-aws-secrets-controller v0.1.0 (/var/lib/buildkite-agent/builds/hetzner-x86-64-16cpu-32gb-doctests-0718a9d7/materialize/test/src/aws-secrets-controller)

_**Agent (model default, mode Agent)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

The rustdoc error says that `[`into_datum`]` is an unresolved link - "no item named `into_datum` in scope". The doc comment is on a method `into_datum_decode_error` which is inside `impl Value`. So when rustdoc resolves `[`into_datum`]` it looks for an item named `into_datum` in scope. In Rust doc, for methods on the same impl block, you typically need to use the full path or `Self::into_datum` to link to another method on the same type.

Let me check - the link [`into_datum`] in the same impl block might need to be [`Value::into_datum`] or we need to use a different format. Actually in rustdoc, when you're in an impl block, [`into_datum`] looks for a function or method named `into_datum` in the current scope. The method is on the same impl, so it might need to be referenced as `Self::into_datum` or the full path `Value::into_datum`.

Looking at Rust documentation, for linking to other methods on the same type from a doc comment, you can use:
- [`into_datum`](Self::into_datum) - links to the method on Self
- Or just use backticks without a link: `into_datum`

So the fix is to change `[`into_datum`]` to `[`Self::into_datum`]` so rustdoc resolves it as the method on the current type (Value).
</details></think>

---


Fixing the intra-doc link: rustdoc can't resolve `[`into_datum`]` in the impl block. Using `[`Self::into_datum`]` so it links to the method on the same type.

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

The cargo doc failed because of rustup not having a default toolchain in this environment, not because of the doc link. The fix I made is the standard way to fix this rustdoc error - changing `[`into_datum`]` to `[`Self::into_datum`]` so that rustdoc can resolve the link to the method on the same type.

---


The doc link is fixed. The failure you saw was from the environment (no default Rust toolchain), not from the doc change.

**Change made:** In `src/pgrepr/src/value.rs`, the doc comment for `into_datum_decode_error` was updated from:

- `[`into_datum`]`  
to  
- `[`Self::into_datum`]`

Rustdoc doesn’t resolve a bare `into_datum` in that impl block. Using `Self::into_datum` makes the link point to the other method on the same type, so the “unresolved link to `into_datum`” error should be gone when you run `cargo doc` (e.g. in CI or with a proper toolchain).
